The method first applies a unified bidirectional reflectance distribution function model (UN-MODEL), suitable for both continuous and discrete canopies, to generate a high-fidelity training dataset. A ResNet ConvLSTM-Attention (RCA) network is then trained on the dataset to learn the relationships between Landsat reflectance and LAI patch time series. The three components of the RCA network combine spatial, temporal, and spectral information through an “Extract–Aggregate–Refine” structure. Results show that the UM-RCA algorithm achieves high accuracy on LAI field measurements across different biome types (overall MAE = 0.510, RMSE = 0.685) and produces more spatiotemporally coherent LAI maps than three mainstream algorithms or products: the Random Forest model, the Sentinel Application Platform (SNAP) biophysical processor, and the High-resolution Global LAnd Surface Satellite (Hi-GLASS) LAI product. The UM-RCA algorithm offers significant practical value for precision agriculture and ecological monitoring.

title: Gait Planning & Adaptive Impedance Control for Turning Walk Based on a Self-Balancing Lower Limb Exoskeleton
authors: Xihao Wang, Shisheng Zhang, Jinke Li, Weijie Sun, Zengle Ren, Wujing Cao, Meng Yin, Xinyu Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Self-balancing lower limb exoskeletons (SBLLE) hold the promise of helping individuals with diverse mobility impairments regain their ability to walk. Equipping exoskeletons with stable turning capabilities is crucial for their real-world application. This paper presents an effective control framework that enables stable turning motions in SBLLE without relying on external assistive devices. A turn-specific gait planner has been designed to generate stable reference gaits. Moreover, an adaptive variable impedance controller (AVIC) is introduced, which adjusts the center of mass (CoM) motion in real time to maintain balance during walking, even when carrying different patients. The proposed approach is validated through simulations, comparative studies, and multi-stage human-subject experiments with ten participants, ranging from healthy users to individuals with severe lower-limb motor impairment. Results consistently demonstrate stable turning performance, with the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) remaining within the support polygon during the entire walking process.

title: Deep reinforcement learning for chaos control of a nonlinear jumping quarter-car model
authors: Masahisa Watanabe
abstract: Vibrations can reduce the controllability of vehicles and can be uncomfortable for passengers. In particular, nonlinear vibrations can lead to larger amplitudes and complex behaviors. Among these vibrations, chaotic vibrations are inherently unpredictable and may lead to instability, which is highly undesirable in practical applications. Although several control methods have been developed to eliminate chaotic vibrations in active suspension systems, they require a priori knowledge about vehicle systems. Thus, designing chaos controllers for practical scenarios can be very challenging. In this study, we propose a method based on machine learning to overcome this limitation. The deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm is used to learn optimal control input to suppress chaotic vibrations. As a typical model of nonlinear motion, we considered a quarter-car model with jumping nonlinearity and trained a reinforcement learning model to suppress chaotic vibrations. The numerical results show that learned policies were able to suppress chaotic vibration and reduce the overall level of vibration. The obtained policies were evaluated across a wide range of forcing frequencies and simulations with stochastic excitation noises. The results demonstrate that chaos control based on deep reinforcement learning was able to reduce vibrations and stabilize chaotic behavior effectively in this application.

title: V-Model Design and Experimental Validation for Robust Direct Power Control of Wind Energy Conversion Systems
authors: Yassine Seghrouchni, Mourad Yessef, Saad Motahhir, Badre Bossoufi, Saad A. Alqahtani, Z.M.S. El-Barbary
abstract: The V-model is a structured engineering framework used in the automotive and aeronautical industries. Each development phase, in this model, corresponds to a specific validation step. This study presents the first of its kind application of the V-model to the wind energy industry, covering the design, implementation, and validation of a direct power control algorithm with proportional-integral (DPC-PI) for a doubly fed induction generator (DFIG). The validation process proceeds through three rigorous stages: Model-in-the-Loop (MIL), Software-in-the-Loop (SIL), and Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL). Under a wind speed step from 6 m/s to 10 m/s, results show that DPC-PI reduces steady-state error by 83.7%, overshoot by 39.4%, and total harmonic distortion (THD) by 92% compared to classical DPC. Next to prove the adaptability of our control to the variations of the environment a conducted parameter variation test demonstrated stable operation and ripple suppression for both active and reactive power. Finally, an assessed operating mode test in sub-synchronous, synchronous, and super-synchronous regions show a reduction of up to 86.4% in power ripples and a THD below 0.9%. Under variable wind conditions, the controller holds THD at 0.84%, outperforming some of the recent methods. In the final phase, the PIL test was executed on the STM32F429 to reveal the real-time performance of this control.

title: SAM-PTx: Text-Guided Fine-Tuning of SAM with Parameter-Efficient, Parallel-Text Adapters
authors: Shayan Jalilian, Abdul Bais
abstract: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) has demonstrated impressive generalization in prompt-based segmentation. Yet, the potential of semantic text prompts remains underexplored compared to traditional spatial prompts, such as points and boxes. This paper introduces SAM-PTx, a parameter-efficient approach for adapting SAM using frozen CLIP-derived text embeddings as class-level semantic guidance. Specifically, we propose a lightweight adapter, the Parallel-Text Adapter, that injects text embeddings into SAM’s image encoder, enabling semantics-guided segmentation while keeping most of the original architecture frozen. Through supervised experiments and ablations on the low-data subsets of COCO and ADE20K as well as the COD10K dataset, we show that incorporating fixed text embeddings as additional inputs improves segmentation performance over purely spatial prompt baselines. To our knowledge, this is the first work to use text prompts for segmentation on the COD10K dataset. These results suggest that integrating semantic conditioning into SAM’s architecture offers a practical, scalable path to efficient adaptation with minimal computational complexity.

title: Towards Efficient and Robust Linguistic Emotion Diagnosis for Mental Health via Multi-Agent Instruction Refinement
authors: Jian Zhang, Zhangqi Wang, Zhiyuan Wang, Weiping Fu, Yu He, Haiping Zhu, Qika Lin, Jun Liu
abstract: Linguistic expressions of emotions, including depression, anxiety, and trauma-related states, are widespread in clinical notes, counseling dialogues, and online mental health communities. Accurate recognition of these emotions is crucial for clinical triage, risk assessment, and timely intervention in mental health related applications. Despite recent advances showing that large language models (LLMs) can generalize well to various emotion analysis tasks, their diagnostic reliability in high-stakes and context-intensive medical settings remains highly sensitive to prompt design. Moreover, existing approaches are challenged by two major issues: emotional comorbidity, where multiple intertwined emotional states complicate prediction, and inefficient exploration of clinically relevant cues. To address these issues, we propose APOLO (Automated Prompt Optimization for Linguistic emOtion diagnosis), a framework that systematically explores a broader and finer-grained prompt space to enhance diagnostic efficiency and robustness. APOLO models instruction refinement as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) and introduces a multi-agent collaboration mechanism comprising the Planner–Teacher–Critic–Student–Target roles. This closed-loop design enables continuous optimization of prompt generation, evaluation, and evolution. After the Planner agent formulates a high-level optimization trajectory within the POMDP framework, the Teacher–Critic–Student agents collaboratively refine the prompts along this trajectory, iteratively enhancing the stability and effectiveness of the reasoning process. Finally, the Target agent determines whether to continue optimization or terminate the search based on performance evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate that APOLO improves diagnostic accuracy and robustness across domain-specific and stratified benchmarks, providing a generalizable and scalable paradigm for trustworthy LLM applications in mental healthcare.

title: Microwave Metasurfaces
authors: Yoshiaki Kasahara, Andrea Alù
abstract: Metasurfaces are planar arrays of subwavelength elements that can efficiently control electromagnetic reflection, refraction, and scattering, being able to replace bulky components with ultrathin, engineered interfaces. This article traces their evolution in the past years with an emphasis on their applications in microwave engineering settings. We feature three classes of microwave metasurfaces: local surfaces, characterized by a tailored surface impedance that enables full control over the phase, amplitude, and polarization of the scattered waves; nonlocal architectures, which have been expanding this paradigm by introducing engineered coupling among the constituent elements of the metasurface and engineered dispersion, enabling angular and spectral selectivity, analog signal processing, and spatial compression; and time-varying and space–time-modulated metasurfaces, which further extend the design space, enabling broadband absorption and magnet-free nonreciprocal propagation, among other features that linear, time-invariant designs cannot support. Finally, digital space–time-coding metasurfaces (STCMs) unify these advances within programmable hardware, offering real-time reconfigurability, multifunctional beam control, and dynamically reconfigurable analog processing. Metasurfaces are rapidly expanding to become an essential tool for microwave engineering, thanks to their agility and ubiquitous relevance in many emerging application spaces.

title: Phase Current Reconstruction for Dual Three-Phase PMSM With Hybrid Zero-Vector Modulation Strategy
authors: Yuhang Song, Yuxuan Du, Wenxiang Zhao, Yunjiang Wu

title: A 2-DOF Modulation Strategy for Quasi-Single-Stage AC-AC Solid-State Transformer with Optimized Current Stress and Extended ZVS Range
authors: Hui Wang, Shuyang Xie, Min Zhou, Jing Guo, Xiaochao Hou, Mei Su
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a single-phase quasi-single-stage back-to-back AC-AC solid-state transformer (SST) topology based on a dual active half-bridge (DAHB) converter. This topology consists of a line frequency (LF) fold bridge on the primary side, a DAHB converter and a LF unfold bridge on the secondary side. The presented topology features reduced number of components and elimination of the twice-frequency pulsating power, offering the advantages of compactness, high power density, and low cost. To address the challenges of high RMS current and loss of ZVS over a wide voltage gain range, this paper designs a hybrid 2-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) modulation scheme for the DAHB converter to achieve ZVS operation and optimized RMS currents. Finally, a 2kW prototype with a peak efficiency of 97.26% is demonstrated, verifying the correctness and feasibility of the presented methods.

title: Neural Learning Control of Fighter Aircraft at High-Angle-of-Attack Maneuvers with Online Recorded Data and Disturbance Observer
authors: Jiashang Yu, Jiaju Yu
abstract: Considering the strong nonlinearities, aerodynamic uncertainties, and external disturbances exhibited by fighter aircrafts during high angle-of-attack (AOA) maneuvers, this article proposes a neural learning control strategy based on online recorded data and a disturbance observer (DOB). The channel coupling is incorporated into the total disturbance, and the model is decomposed into the AOA, sideslip, and roll-rate subsystems. The neural network approximates system uncertainties, while an online recorded data scheme constructs the prediction error combined with the tracking error to form the adaptive law. On this basis, the DOB is designed to estimate composite disturbances in real time and incorporated into the online recorded data model as a feedforward compensation to obtain the desired control moments. An allocation optimization model is further established to compute aerodynamic control surface deflections and thrust vectoring angles online. Lyapunov analysis proves the uniform ultimate boundedness of the closed loop system. Finally, simulations of the Herbst maneuver under disturbance torques verify that the proposed strategy achieves high learning performance and accurate tracking of maneuver commands.

title: A BOOSTER PACK FOR IT EDUCATION: A Program Evaluation for Information Technology Education in Higher Education Institutions
authors: Noel E. Estrella, Rosalyn Galvez
publish: 2025 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence, Data Engineering and Sciences (ICAIDES)
abstract: This study evaluates the Information Technology Education (ITE) program in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs), integrating both quantitative and qualitative data to identify key improvement areas. The research employed a Sequential Explanatory Mixed Methods design, utilizing the PAASCU evaluation tool and stakeholder feedback through surveys and interviews. Quantitative data revealed high ratings from administrators and industry experts, indicating strong alignment with institutional goals and industry standards. However, lower scores from students and alumni highlighted gaps in student engagement and real-world applicability. Qualitative insights emphasized the need for increased practical assessments, integration of emerging technologies, and enhanced soft skills training. Key improvement areas identified include increased practical assessments, student engagement in program development, integration of emerging technologies, enhanced soft skills training, regular program evaluation, and increased industry collaboration. The findings underscore the necessity of a comprehensive and adaptive program that serves as a necessary “Booster Pack” to meet the rapidly evolving demands of the global IT industry and student needs.

title: Multi-modal Collaboration-based 5G-UWB Fusion Method for High-Precision Industrial Positioning
authors: Zhirun Deng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing and Communication Technology (SPCT)
abstract: Accurate positioning in complex industrial environments remains challenging due to multipath interference, signal occlusion, and hardware asynchrony. This paper introduces a hybrid 5G-UWB multimodal positioning framework that combines synchronized signal acquisition, deep feature fusion, and federated optimization. We first employ a timestamp calibration protocol to align the heterogeneous 5G and UWB systems, enabling the synchronized collection of TDOA, beamforming, and CIR data while mitigating inter-device clock drift. The core fusion model is a multimodal LSTM-CNN network featuring the Reliability-Aware Adaptive Attention Fusion (RAAF) mechanism. RAAF extracts temporal and spatial features and incorporates a reliability estimator that dynamically weights each modality’s contribution based on real-time signal quality and occlusion like-lihood. Furthermore, to improve scalability, privacy protection, and energy efficiency, the framework incorporates a federated learning strategy with integrated differential privacy. This allows lightweight local submodels to be trained on terminals and aggregated adaptively. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves accuracy and robustness over single-modality baselines, achieving centimeter-level precision even in dynamic, obstructed, and resource-limited industrial scenarios.

title: Field Application and Condition Monitoring of Natural Ester Transformers in High-Altitude Power Systems
authors: Zongbo Yu, Jingde Liu, Chao Li, Zhijun Zhao, Jian Li, Dongyang Zhao, Haoxi Cong, Xuefeng Hu
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: A natural ester-filled distribution transformer was deployed and operated under real grid-connected conditions in a high-altitude region of the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau to evaluate its field performance and environmental adaptability. The transformer was monitored using online dissolved gas analysis, high-frequency partial discharge detection, and long-term temperature measurement to assess the chemical, electrical, and thermal behavior of the insulation system during service. Field results show that key fault-related gas concentrations remained stable without abnormal accumulation, and seasonal temperature variations had a limited impact on gas generation characteristics, with the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO} / \text{CO}_{2}$</tex> ratio indicating normal thermal aging of cellulose insulation. No detectable partial discharge activity was observed throughout the monitoring period, and temperature records demonstrate stable thermal performance and effective oil circulation even under low ambient temperature conditions. These results provide field evidence supporting the safe and reliable application of natural ester transformers in highaltitude power systems.

title: Using the Kalman Filter in Sensorless Control System by Doubly Fed Machine
authors: Oleh Kliuiev, Oleksandr Sadovoi, Serhii Serhiienko, Yuliya Sokhina
publish: 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Modern Electrical and Energy System (MEES)
abstract: This paper research the dynamics of a sensorless control system for a doubly fed machine (DFM) implemented on the basis of a Kalman filter (KF). A new form of writing the KF equations is obtained due to the fact that the stator voltage vector is considered as a state variable depending on the angle rotation of the rotor, and not as an input action. The Jacobi matrix of observer contains partial derivatives of the projections of the stator voltage vector by the rotor rotation angle. These derivatives introduce a preemption into the process of identifying coordinates by the Kalman filter which made it possible to avoid self-oscillations in the control system that arose when using the standard approach, when stator voltages are considered as external influences. With the proposed structure of the KF matrices it was possible to avoid self-oscillations in the control system and ensure asymptotic stability with sufficient identification accuracy. For the first time in one control system it was possible to combine direct relay control in the DFM channels and the KF in the feedback circuit, which perceives minor high-frequency pulsations of rotor currents at the fundamental harmonic, arising during relay control, as random interference. This gives stability to the KF calculation algorithm.

title: Mobile Robot Inspection Path Planning Based on Multi-agent Co-Evolutionary Algorithm
authors: Yan Zeng, Zhuo Song, Fengming Ren, Mianheng Lai, Guozhong Cheng, Ke Ke
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)
abstract: Efficient on-site monitoring is critical to ensure that construction progress aligns with Building Information Modeling (BIM) plans. However, achieving complete and consistent data acquisition in large and complex environments remains challenging. Manual inspection is labor-intensive and error-prone, while existing robotic exploration approaches often suffer from limited coverage and low efficiency. This paper presents a multi-agent cooperative inspection planning framework for autonomous construction monitoring. The proposed method employs a waypoint-based planning pipeline that enables the inspection robot to collaboratively achieve full coverage of construction surfaces. Specifically, a bi-level waypoint optimization strategy is formulated to jointly address inspection site generation and reduction under explicit coverage constraints. To address the problem's discrete, coupled nature, a cooperative evolutionary algorithm is developed to generate feasible, high-quality waypoint sets. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly reduces inspection time and path length while ensuring complete coverage and high data consistency compared with conventional planners.

title: Research on Control Technology of Unmanned “Ant Boat” for Complex Scenarios
authors: Jinde Huang, Jing Zeng, Zhulang Li, Bohua Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: This study addresses the challenges in marine development posed by traditional ship equipment, such as high costs, single-function limitations, environmental pollution, and difficulties in dismantling. We propose an unmanned “Ant Boat” swarm control system based on modular design. The system employs an STM32F407ZGT6 microcontroller as the core controller, integrating Beidou RDSS/RNSS positioning modules, magnetic adsorption coil modules, intelligent fixing buckle modules, high-precision MG996 controllable rotating servo modules, waterproof propulsion motor modules, and collaborative control algorithms. This enables autonomous docking, path planning, and multi-configuration platform construction for unmanned boats. The system provides a green and efficient solution for complex scenarios such as offshore temporary bases and mobile entertainment platforms.

title: Cooperative Work Simulation of Construction Equipment using Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Guozhong Cheng, Ke Ke, Zhuo Song, Dehui Lu, Lufeng Wang
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)
abstract: Different types of construction equipment engage in complex cooperative interactions on construction sites, significantly influencing project progress. This study addresses the problem of cooperative excavation involving multiple heterogeneous machines—such as excavators and bulldozers—within a multi-agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) framework. A parameter-sharing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) architecture is introduced in a grid-world simulation environment, where all agents share a unified policy network and learn coordinated strategies through end-to-end training. To promote effective collaboration and address exploration challenges, a dense reward structure is designed, incorporating incentives for task completion, spoil delivery, excavation chain formation, and depth progression. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves full cooperation within 2 minutes in small environments. As the scale increases, runtime grows approximately linearly and completion drops to 0.85.

title: A Two-Stage Framework for Interpreting Time Series Model Outputs with Large Language Models
authors: Tianze Deng, Zengni Zhang, Yuhan Dong, Kai Zhang
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)
abstract: Time series analysis, which encompasses tasks such as forecasting, imputation, classification, and anomaly detection, plays a pivotal role in many fields including energy, transportation, and healthcare. Although deep learning models have achieved remarkable predictive performance, their inherent opacity presents significant challenges in domains requiring transparent decision-making processes. To bridge this gap, we introduce an innovative framework that harnesses the capabilities of LLMs to interpret the outputs of time series models. Our methodology operates through a two-phase approach: initially deploying SOTA time-series models for precise prediction generation, followed by the application of LLMs to produce comprehensible explanations of these outcomes. In particular, we systematically exploit the temporal metadata embedded within time series data, including temporal indicators such as time of day and seasonal patterns, to enhance the interpretability and facilitate a deeper understanding of the underlying predictive dynamics. Moreover, we added reverse normalization to reinstate the original physical characteristics of the data, thereby enhancing the logical coherence of the LLM-generated explanations. Extensive validation across multiple datasets demonstrates the consistent delivery of meaningful and interpretable insights, positioning our approach as particularly valuable for applications where transparency is paramount.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning Based on Heterogeneous Graph mixed-head attention for flexible job-shop scheduling problem
authors: Yuhan Huang, Zi-Qi Zhang, Bin Qian, Rong Hu, Wei Chen, Wei-Cheng Gao
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)
abstract: In recent years, graph neural network (GNN) based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods has become a popular tool for complex scheduling. As an extension of the job shop scheduling problems (JSPs), in flexible JSP (FJSPs), the conventional disjunctive-graph formulation struggles to reflect operation–machine matching and resource contention, and uniform multi-head attention cannot reflect heterogeneous node difficulty. This paper proposes a heterogeneous GNN (HGNN) based on a mixed-head attention mechanism called heterogeneous graph mixed-head attention (HGMHA). By constructing a heterogeneous graph containing both operation and machine nodes, and designing a node complexity evaluation function called learning feature-aware head allocation network (LFHAN) to dynamically allocate the number of attention heads, adaptive optimization of computational resources is achieved. This approach enables differentiated allocation of computational resources based on the complexity of operational features, improving model efficiency. On standard benchmarks, HGMHA reduces makespan and runtime comparing with dispatching rules, classical meta heuristics, and prior DRL baselines, validating the mixed head design.

title: Short-Term Power Load Forecasting Based on Signal Decomposition and Correlation Analysis
authors: Hongtao Wang, Jiacai Han
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: To address the nonlinear, non-stationary, and highly fluctuating characteristics of power load sequences, this paper proposes a hybrid neural network model that integrates Improved Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD), Pearson Correlation Analysis, IVY Optimization Algorithm, and a CNN-Transformer framework for load forecasting. The model first adapts the number of Intrinsic Mode Functions (IMFs) in the VMD decomposition process by using Pearson Correlation Coefficients (PCC), ensuring complementary information between modes and reducing mode mixing. Subsequently, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is employed to extract local temporal features, which are then processed by a Transformer module to capture long-range dependencies, effectively capturing both short-term fluctuations and long-term trends in the power load signal. Based on this, the model's key hyperparameters are adaptively optimized using an improved IVY Optimization Algorithm. This algorithm enhances the global search capability and convergence speed by incorporating dynamic parameter adjustment, chaotic initialization, and elite retention strategies. Experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing deep learning networks in terms of prediction performance and stability, demonstrating its significant advantages and practical value in power load forecasting.

title: Implementation of a Generative Model: RGB-To-NDVI Image Translation for Monitoring the Vegetation Health Index of Rice Crops
authors: Nurfadhilah Mardianti Andini, Mike Yuliana, Haryadi Amran Darwito
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Information System and Information Technology (ICISIT)
abstract: Pest and disease attacks significantly affect rice productivity. To address this, drones were utilized for rapid crop-health monitoring. However, NDVI images traditionally require expensive multispectral cameras. Therefore, we adapt and implement the CycleGAN method introduced by Zhu et al. (2017) for a new context converting agricultural drone RGB imagery into NDVI to support plant health analysis. This approach eliminates the need for costly multispectral cameras, as it only requires a standard RGB camera. CycleGAN is a deep learning architecture that translates images from one domain to another without requiring paired image-to-image data. The conversion of RGB images to NDVI in this study achieved a histogram correlation score of 0.9672, indicating a high degree of similarity and demonstrating the reliability of the proposed method. The final result of this research is the analysis of plant vegetation levels using RGB images, with the output projected onto a map that includes location-related information.

title: Key Technologies of Integrated Gate Driving and Online Collector-Emitter Voltage Monitoring for High-Power IGBTs
authors: Guowei Zhou, Jingen Hu, Ziyun Zhao
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: To enhance the operational reliability and state visualization of power devices in hybrid DC circuit breakers, this study proposes a highly integrated circuit solution that combines IGBT gate driving and on-line collector-emitter voltage monitoring functions. The solution adopts a push-pull driving structure with dual independent power supplies, enabling adjustable gate voltage output within the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15-35 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex> range, and integrates optical isolation, signal amplification, and collector-emitter voltage monitoring modules on a single PCB. The collector-emitter voltage monitoring module uses a small current source to bias diodes, combined with a differential sampling circuit based on an operational amplifier, to achieve real-time acquisition and measurement of the turn-on voltage drop. An auxiliary MOSFET branch provides current diversion and high-voltage isolation during turn-off, enhancing measurement safety and anti-interference capability. Experimental results show that the integrated circuit can stably achieve IGBT driving and real-time voltage drop monitoring under typical hybrid DC circuit breaker operating conditions. It features a compact structure, fast response, high measurement consistency, and low cost, providing a feasible technical solution for IGBT health monitoring and highly reliable hybrid DC circuit breaker systems.

title: Study on Temperature Effect of Remote Module in Optical Measurement System of DC Centralized Monitoring and Control Converter Station
authors: Xinrui Chen, Wenxin Feng, Hao Liu, Zhiyuan Qiu, Pei Zhou, Ning Yu, Fangbo Xing, Zhongyu Feng
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: The HVDC (High Voltage Direct Current) Centralized Monitoring and Control Converter Station is a popular research direction in current DC projects. To ensure the stability and reliability of the centralized monitoring and control function, higher requirements are put forward for the accuracy and reliability of the optical measurement system. The remote module in the high-voltage converter station optical measurement system is the core equipment, but its failure rate is obviously high during the actual operation, which seriously affects the operational reliability of the HVDC transmission system. In this paper, for the remote module of Siemens OPTODYN D, the remote module temperature effect platform is built to calculate the variation of each specific parameter in the transmission protocol of the remote module under different temperature conditions, so that the influence of temperature effect on the operating state of the remote module is obtained through comprehensive analysis. The experimental results show that as the temperature increases, the amplitude of the output pulse of the remote module decreases, and the rise time, fall time and pulse width of the pulse waveform increase, resulting in lower reliability and higher failure rate of the remote module.

title: Proposing Deep Learning Models for Vietnamese Medical Prescription Named Entity Recognition
authors: Dung Lai Tri, Bang Nguyen Anh, Hiep Van Hoang, Phat Huu Nguyen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence, Data Engineering and Sciences (ICAIDES)
abstract: Automated information extraction from medical prescriptions is a critical yet challenging task due to the diversity in document layouts and the need for high accuracy. This paper addresses the task of Named Entity Recognition (NER) for Vietnamese medical prescriptions by evaluating and comparing three advanced deep learning architectures. We establish a baseline using a fine-tuned LayoutLMv3 [1] model and investigate two hybrid graph-based approaches, GAT and Graphormer, which explicitly model the spatial relationships between text segments. A key aspect of our methodology is a two-stage fine-tuning strategy, leveraging the synthetic FATURA [2] dataset to instill a general understanding of document structure before specializing on the target VAIPE-P [3] prescription dataset. Our comprehensive experiments reveal that the Graphormer architecture achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching a micro-F1 score of 99.66%.

title: Redesigning the Undergraduate Program for Enhanced Talent Cultivation in Smart Grid Information Engineering
authors: Minghang Zhong, Jianning Yin, Haiyan Wang, Xinghua Liu, Xiangqian Tong, Jiandong Duan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power System and Renewable Energy (PSRE)
abstract: During the revision of the undergraduate program for Smart Grid Information Engineering, several issues were identified, including a lack of distinctive training objectives, curriculum homogenization, and insufficient development of students' practical abilities. This paper analyzes these challenges by examining discipline positioning, course structure, and industry-academia collaboration. Building on revision experiences from multiple smart grid programs, it presents a student-centered approach to program redesign. Key recommendations include enhancing practical and internship components, reforming the existing curriculum, and evaluating the efficacy of the revised plan. This redesigned program aligns with the evolving needs of undergraduate education, aims to improve teaching quality, and contributes to cultivating the high-quality scientific and technological workforce required for national development.

title: Inertia Assessment and Lifting Control Technology for Offshore Wind Farms
authors: Junyang Zhang, Zheng Li, Cheng Peng, Xu Sun, Yijing Chen, Xiaojiang Guo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power System and Renewable Energy (PSRE)
abstract: This paper presents the latest research outcomes in the domain of inertia assessment and control for offshore wind farms. It first elaborates on the inertia composition of offshore wind power evacuation systems via high-voltage flexible transmission and proposes a calculation methodology for the system equivalent inertia. Taking actual wind turbine units as case studies, the equivalent inertia time constants under various operating conditions are verified through tests. Moreover, a control strategy for enhancing the inertia of offshore wind farms is put forward, and a marine communication architecture capable of rapidly responding to the system's active support requirements is designed, with the time duration of each communication link measured in practical scenarios. Eventually, a low-latency active support control response architecture is established, which enables long-distance and multi-level collaborative coordination among large-scale wind turbine units, offshore wind farms, highvoltage flexible transmission systems, and the power grid.

title: An Rl-Snn Based Approach for Reserve Forecasting and Unit Commitment Optimization
authors: Shi Wang, Xuning Tan, Huanxin Liao, Tianze Yu, Guolong Liu, Junhua Zhao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power System and Renewable Energy (PSRE)
abstract: In high-penetration renewable energy systems, the inherent uncertainty of inverter-based resources exacerbates power imbalance risks in the Unit Commitment (UC) problem. Existing fixed-proportion spinning reserve allocation methods struggle to balance economy and stability, while lacking assessment tools that couple reserve prediction with system security. To address this issue, this paper proposes a reserve prediction and unit commitment security assessment method based on Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Stochastic Neural Network (SNN). The method establishes a novel unit commitment scheduling framework that integrates RL-based day-ahead prediction, SNN algorithms, and convex optimization-based intra-day scheduling. Specifically, the RL intelligent decision-maker generates highlevel decisions such as unit commitment and spinning reserve requirements; the SNN is responsible for training stochastic hidden neurons within the RL algorithm; subsequently, the convex optimization executor solves the intra-day economic dispatch problem based on the reserve quantity predicted by RL, and feeds the calculated expected total cost back to RL as performance signals to refine its decision strategy, achieving closed-loop optimization. Based on this, a novel security assessment method is established that considers reserve quantity magnitude. Simulation experiments on IEEE 39-bus power system verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Grid-level Indicator for Unlabeled Instance Discrimination in Few-shot Object Detection
authors: Taijin Zhao, Linfeng Xu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)
abstract: Few-Shot Object Detection (FSOD) faces significant performance degradation due to incomplete annotations, where unlabeled foreground instances are incorrectly treated as background during training. Existing mitigation strategies often suppress high-scoring negative proposals indiscriminately, failing to distinguish false negatives from true hard negatives. To address this, we propose a Grid-level Indicator (GI) that identifies false negatives by analyzing the distribution of class activation responses and class predictions. Specifically, GI identifies a proposal as a false negative if its high response originates from outside the annotated region or if it is an in-box proposal that responds strongly to a different foreground class. The recognized false negatives are excluded from training to refine detector optimization. Experiments on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO show our method achieves significant improvements over the baseline.

title: Design of Combined Electromagnetic Silence of Power Lines Magnetic Fields based on Geometric Inverse Magnetic Field Problem Solution
authors: Ihor Bovdui, Borys Kuznetsov, Tatyana Nikitina, Kostiantyn Chunikhin, Olena Voloshko, Valerij Kolomiets
publish: 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Modern Electrical and Energy System (MEES)
abstract: The purpose of the work is to develop a method for designing of combined electromagnetic silence (CEMS) including active and solid passive parts of magnetic field (MF) generated by overhead power lines (OPL). CEMS consists of double-circuit system of active silence (SAS) and U-shaped solid passive silence (SPS), and characterized by increased initial MF level reducing efficiency. CEMS designing reduced to solving the geometric inverse problem (GIP) of magneto-quasi-static’s (MQS) based on minimax vector optimization problem (MMVOP) solution, in which vector objective function (VOF) calculated based on of three-dimensional Maxwell's equations (TDME) solutions in quasi-stationary approximation (QSA) using the COMSOL Multiphysics software package. MMVOP solution calculated based on multi-particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithms from Pareto-optimal solutions taking into account binary preference relations. During CEMS design spatial location coordinates of SAS two compensation winding (CW), winding currents magnitudes and phases calculated, as well as SPS U-shaped parameters calculated. TDME theoretical modeling and experimental studies of designed CEMS effectiveness MF generated by OPL in old residential buildings inside were carried out.

title: In Silico Classification of Nanotoxicity Using QSAR-Perturbation Based Model
authors: Heidi A. Puato, Perlita E. Gasmen, Jahaziel B. Rafael, Aries P. Valeriano, John Riz V. Bagnol, Vince Jebryl G. Montero, Loreniel E. Añonuevo, Rey Y. Capangpangan
abstract: Nanoparticles (NPs) offer numerous benefits across biomedicine, physics, chemistry, and agriculture, but due to their high surface-to-volume ratio can also lead to toxic effects, posing risks to biological systems and ecosystems. Nanotoxicity testing is essential for assessing the risks of NPs, but experimental assays are often time- and resource-intensive. In-silico methods provide a more efficient alternative. These computation methods are usually centered around the quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) modeling. In this paper, a refined method for the predictive capacity of QSAR modeling is proposed by integrating the Perturbation Theory. Using different machine learning (ML) algorithms, this study developed a QSAR-perturbation model to predict toxicity profiles of NPs under diverse experimental conditions. Experiments show very promising performance from XGBoost with Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) compared to other ML models. Feature correlation and Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) were also applied to enhance the explainability of the models.

title: PDM-YOLOV11-Based PCBA Defect Detection Method
authors: Xinyuan Wang, Wuzhou Wen
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Intelligent Communication, Sensing and Electromagnetics (ICSE)
abstract: To address the problems of high cost of detection equipment and non-standardized defects in Printed Circuit Board Assembly (PCBA) defect detection methods, this paper proposes a PCBA defect detection method based on PDM-YOLOv11. To improve the accuracy of PCBA defect detection, an improved YOLOv11(You Only Look Once) recognition method named PDM-YOLOv11 is proposed. Firstly, the C3k2 module is fused with the DualConv module. By combining 3×3 and 1×1 convolutional kernels, efficient information flow and integration among different feature map channels are achieved, which maintains network depth and representation capability, reduces computational complexity and model size, and makes it more suitable for resource-constrained environments. To further enhance the model's detection accuracy for PCBA defects, the Context Anchor Attention (CAA) mechanism is introduced to improve localization precision. Experimental results verify that the proposed PDM-YOLOv11 method achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 83.4% in PCBA defect detection tasks, which is 3.9 percentage points higher than that of YOLOv11. This result indicates that the improved model has significant advantages in object detection performance and provides a practical and reliable technical solution for PCBA defect detection.

title: Design of Efficient Thermoelectric Modules Using Damped Semiconductor Rod Structures
authors: Oleksandr Kyrychenko, Sergey Taranenko, Olha Melnyk, Yevhen Vereshchago, Vitalii Kostiuchenko
publish: 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Modern Electrical and Energy System (MEES)
abstract: The research highlights the advantages for designed efficient thermoelectric modules with damped structural configurations of semiconductor rods. To facilitate a comparative assessment for performance, six diverse thermoelectric module models were developed, each characterized by a «U-shaped snake» comprising 17 P-N semiconductor pairs. The first four models with structural configurations of semiconductor rods were evaluated against the fifth and sixth models, which utilized cubic semiconductor shapes, as well as in relation to one another. The first and fifth models lacked damping components and had overall dimensions of 35×27.5×7 mm. In contrast, the second, third, fourth, and sixth models with overall dimensions of 35×27.5×9 mm included single-layer dampers crafted from plasticity material of varying sizes. From the analyses of these six module designs, the required performance characteristics were generated, resulting in a detailed overview of the key operational parameters.

title: Optimal NIPT Timing Selection and Fetal Abnormality Detection Based on Clustering Multi-Objective Optimization and Machine Learning
authors: Jiale Xu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) is a crucial method for early screening of fetal chromosomal abnormalities, with detection accuracy significantly influenced by gestational age and maternal body mass index (BMI). This study addresses two core issues: optimal NIPT detection timing for different maternal BMI groups and abnormality detection in female fetuses. Based on 1,377 clinical samples (772 male fetuses, 605 female fetuses), we propose an integrated framework combining clustering analysis, multi-objective optimization, and machine learning. For male fetuses, K-means clustering algorithm is employed for BMI-based grouping, with expected risk functions and multi-objective optimization determining personalized detection timing. For female fetuses, a decision tree-based classification model is constructed for abnormality detection. Results demonstrate: Kmeans clustering (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K=3$</tex>) achieves a silhouette coefficient of 0.5907; the BMI grouping strategy yields <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30-40 \%$</tex> detection success rates at optimal timing (12-14 weeks), with multi-factor optimization improving to over 90 %; the female fetal abnormality detection model achieves AUC of 0.6356 and recall of 0.3654. Monte Carlo simulation validates model robustness, providing theoretical foundation and practical guidance for personalized prenatal screening protocols.

title: A Model-Driven Approach to Boost Soybean Productivity and Efficiency
authors: Erma Suryani, Arif Wibisono, Mudjahidin Mudjahidin, Alifia Az Zahra, Rahmatika Jagad Pramundito, Hajid Alauddin Ramadhan, Rafief Chalvani, Gunawan Gunawan, Denny Kurniawan
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Information System and Information Technology (ICISIT)
abstract: This study develops a system dynamics model to address challenges in soybean productivity and efficiency within Indonesia’s agricultural sector, where domestic production struggles to meet rising demand. The model comprehensively integrates production inputs, harvest area dynamics, market demand, and post-harvest distribution processes to identify effective policy interventions. In this study, two scenarios are used: the implementation of superior seeds and efficient loss reduction in post-harvest handling. The impact of implementing superior seeds can significantly increase soybean productivity, which is projected to reach 1.78 tons per hectare in 2028 and approximately 2.4 tons per hectare in 2040. Implementing efficiency measures—such as efficient post-harvest handling (e.g., hermetic bags and FIFO stock rotation)—significantly mitigates losses and sustains higher production levels. This research offers an analytical foundation for integrated and responsive policies aimed at fostering a more resilient and sustainable soybean agricultural system.

title: Can an LLM Induce a Graph? Investigating Memory Drift and Context Length
authors: Raquib Bin Yousuf, Aadyant Khatri, Shengzhe Xu, Mandar Sharma, Naren Ramakrishnan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Knowledge Graph (ICKG)
abstract: Recently proposed evaluation benchmarks aim to characterize the effective context length and the forgetting tendencies of large language models (LLMs). However, these benchmarks often rely on simplistic “needle in a haystack” retrieval or continuation tasks that may not accurately reflect the performance of these models in information-dense scenarios. Thus, rather than simple next token prediction, we argue for evaluating these models on more complex reasoning tasks that requires them to induce structured relational knowledge from the text - such as graphs from potentially noisy natural language content. While the input text can be viewed as generated in terms of a graph, its structure is not made explicit and connections must be induced from distributed textual cues, separated by long contexts and interspersed with irrelevant information. Our findings reveal that LLMs begin to exhibit memory drift and contextual forgetting at much shorter effective lengths when tasked with this form of relational reasoning, compared to what existing benchmarks suggest. With these findings, we offer recommendations for the optimal use of popular LLMs for complex reasoning tasks. We further show that even models specialized for reasoning, such as OpenAI o1, remain vulnerable to early memory drift in these settings. These results point to significant limitations in the models' ability to abstract structured knowledge from unstructured input and highlight the need for architectural adaptations to improve long-range reasoning. Our codebase to support reproducibility is publicly available.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://github.com/DiscoveryAnalyticsCenter/MemoryDrift.

title: Force Sensorless Admittance Control for Physical Human–Robot Interaction with Direct-Drive Manipulators
authors: Yang Liu, Xiaojuan Wang, Cheng Xie, Yuxiang He, Jiabao Geng, Songlin Chen
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)

title: PRISM: A Framework for Multi-Level Modeling and Analysis of Polarization Knowledge
authors: Demetris Paschalides, George Pallis, Marios D. Dikaiakos
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Knowledge Graph (ICKG)
abstract: Polarization poses a growing threat to democratic discourse, public trust, and societal stability. To better understand its structure and evolution, we conceptualize polarization as a multi-level phenomenon spanning entities, groups, and topics. We present PRISM, a framework that models polarization using a typed, weighted, and directed structure known as the Polarization Knowledge Graph (PKG). PRISM introduces a suite of analytical methods for multi-level analysis: i) identifying key actors and categorizing them as protagonists or antagonists based on their contribution to conflict, ii) measuring group cohesiveness through ideological alignment and topic-level agreement, and iii) ranking topics by their polarization intensity. We validate PRISM through a case study on U.S. COVID-19 media discourse, uncovering polarization patterns that align with established findings and highlight the politicization of the pandemic.

title: CausalProto-CLIP:Fine-Grained and Interpretable Vision-Language Modeling via Causal Prototypes
authors: Yifan Meng, Qiuyuan Wei, Zirui Pei, Peng Miao, Chaohong Tan, Zuqiang Meng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: Although Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models demonstrate exceptional performance in downstream tasks, their decision-making processes exhibit significant limitations in fine-grained part-level interpretability, with existing methods predominantly focusing on global features and struggling to precisely localize critical discriminative regions. To address this challenge, this paper proposes CausalProto-CLIP(CP-CLIP), an innovative framework that enhances both the fine-grained classification capability and interpretability of CLIP models through causal prototype-guided mechanisms. The framework consists of two main modules: Causal Negative Prototypes (CNP) for prototype-guided fine-tuning and CausalProto Chain (CPC) for interpretability. It employs a causal prototype extraction strategy to construct robust part-level representations from training images, using adaptive thresholding and causal intervention testing to identify positive prototypes with significant impacts on class predictions. The CNP module innovatively generates negative prototypes through controlled perturbations and causal validation, integrating them into the contrastive learning process with additional alignment and negative contrastive losses to reinforce causal reasoning, thereby significantly boosting the model's discriminative power, robustness, and generalization. Complementing this, the CPC module provides flexible interpretability through region detection, prototype matching, enhanced causal analysis with do-calculus and backdoor adjustment, and a trustworthiness scoring system, achieving a breakthrough from coarse-grained global explanations to finegrained part-level decision tracing with causal graphs. Experiments demonstrate that CP-CLIP maintains excellent classi-fication performance while precisely localizing decision-critical components, revealing how CLIP models utilize local features and providing crucial transparency for safety-critical deployment scenarios.

title: The Impact of Body Fat on the Signal Performance of Implantable NFC Chips
authors: Ye Zhang, Xiaodong Song, Mao Wang, Megumi Saito, Shigeru Shimamoto
abstract: With the rapid development of implantable Near-Field Communication (NFC) technology in fields such as healthcare and identity verification, a thorough understanding of its signal propagation characteristics in various human tissue environments has become increasingly critical. This study investigates the influence of fat tissue on the signal performance of implantable NFC systems. A simulation model was established using the Ansys High-Frequency Structure Simulator (HFSS), incorporating fat layers of varying thicknesses and two planar spiral antennas representing the tag and reader, respectively. The return loss (RL) was calculated from the simulation results to evaluate the transmission efficiency of the system. The results demonstrate that increasing fat thickness leads to a significant rise in RL values, indicating a degradation in signal transmission efficiency due to the presence of adipose tissue. These findings provide a theoretical foundation for enhancing the adaptability and stability of implantable NFC systems across individuals with varying body fat compositions.

title: Simulation and Experimental Study on the Diffusion Characteristics of Characteristic Gases Inside Oil Filled Submarine Cables
authors: Zhifeng Liu, Jianping Liao, Mingchun Hou, Fan Gao, Qingsong Liu, Yufei Chen, Yongye Xu, Haozhen Yang
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: Oil filled submarine cables have been fully utilized in long-distance, high-capacity, and high-voltage transmission projects, but the “breathing” limited flow characteristics of its internal insulation oil have led to unclear diffusion laws of fault characteristic gases, which seriously restricts the development of its operation and maintenance technology. This article comprehensively uses molecular dynamics simulation, finite element analysis, and experimental verification to systematically study the diffusion behavior of seven characteristic gases, including <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{H}_{2}, \text{CH}_{4}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$</tex> in alkylbenzene insulating oil. The research results show that the diffusion coefficient of characteristic gases in alkylbenzene oil is in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10^{-7} ~\text{cm}^{2} \cdot ~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$</tex>, and their diffusion ability is in the order of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{H}_{2}>\text{CO}_{2}>\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}>\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}>\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}>\text{CO}>\text{CH}_{4}$</tex>, and the diffusion coefficient is sensitive to temperature changes but not affected by gas concentration; Finite element simulation further reveals that even under local overheating faults at 550° C, the temperature rise inside the oil passage is limited, and the overall temperature field distribution is uniform; Experimental verification confirms that gas diffusion in static oil is slow, while oil flow drives gas to migrate rapidly in the form of “wave packets”, and its concentration shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing along the diffusion path. This article reveals the diffusion mechanism of characteristic gases in oil filled submarine cables, providing technical support for their effective monitoring and precise positioning.

title: Multi-Area Tie-Line Optimal Scheduling Method for Power Systems with High Penetration of Renewables
authors: Yixuan He, Kunyu Wang, Kai Jiang, Jie Huang, Zhen Wang, Nian Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power System and Renewable Energy (PSRE)
abstract: The grid connection scale of new energy sources such as wind power and photovoltaics is expanding, creating an urgent demand for cross-regional coordinated power dispatch. As a key link connecting regional power grids and balancing power surpluses and shortages, the rationality of tie-line dispatch directly affects cross-regional power supply security, new energy absorption efficiency, and system economy. The volatility and intermittency of high-penetration new energy have raised higher requirements for tie-line dispatch, yet existing methods have shortcomings. Specifically, the modeling of new energy output fails to fully capture the coupling of meteorological factors and the heterogeneity of output among power stations within a region. In multi-area dispatch, overall optimization is prioritized while differences in transmission sections within regions are overlooked. This often results in dispatch schemes violating section constraints, leading to poor adaptability and feasibility. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multi-area tie-line optimal dispatch method for such systems. It achieves refined modeling of new energy output by virtue of high-precision meteorological data, constructs power supply and absorption scenarios based on the spatiotemporal distribution of new energy output, and presents a multi-scenario and multi-section regionalized external transmission curve optimization method that balances regional power balance and section constraints. Verified on the modified IEEE 118-bus system, this method effectively improves power supply guarantee capacity and new energy absorption. It can quickly generate revised tie-line schemes adapted to sections under extreme scenarios, and enhances the adaptability and feasibility of dispatch schemes significantly.

title: Evolution Mechanism of Surface Discharge Characteristics of Power Device Insulation Packaging
authors: Xinpei Wang, Yingjie Guo, Xianfeng Li, Jian Wang, Hanwen Ren, Ikromjon Usmonovich Rakhmonov
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: Surface discharge is prone to occur at the interface between silicone gel and ceramic substrates in power modules, which can markedly degrade the insulation reliability of the device. In view of this, this study focuses on the discharge behavior of adhesive-solid insulation subjected to highfrequency electrical stress. Taking into account the solid-liquid biphasic nature and the self-healing capability of silicone gel, the work proposes an alternative perspective by linking surface discharge to bubble evolution, and further provides a systematic discussion of the failure patterns and damage mechanisms of adhesive-solid insulation. The results indicate that discharge pulses are primarily concentrated after voltage polarity reversal and around the voltage peak, while discharge-related parameters show a frequency-dependent trend that increases initially and then decreases. These findings offer useful theoretical guidance for optimizing insulation structure design in IGBT packaging.

title: Application of Space-Air-Ground Integrated Technology in Slope Instability Risk Early Warning for HVDC Transmission Towers in Complex Terrain of Guangxi
authors: Guogen Li
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: Guangxi is dominated by mountains and hills, featuring complex geological conditions and concentrated rainfall. HVDC transmission towers in this region frequently face safety risks such as slope collapse, foundation sliding, and tower collapse, which seriously threaten the stable operation of the HVDC transmission system. Aiming at the limitations of traditional survey and early warning methods, such as low efficiency, limited coverage, and insufficient recognition accuracy, this paper proposes a refined early warning scheme for slope instability risks based on space-air-ground integrated technology. The scheme integrates multisource technologies including UAV remote sensing, satellite time-series deformation monitoring, combined geophysical exploration, and targeted drilling to construct a full-process technical system of “space-air monitoring for target area delineation → ground survey for precise identification → intelligent model for risk early warning”. SBAS-InSAR technology is adopted for wide-area surface deformation monitoring, and UAV multi-sensor fusion is used to obtain refined geological characteristics of slopes. Key parameters are verified by combining combined geophysical exploration with targeted drilling, and finally a multi-dimensional risk assessment model is established to realize early prediction and precise prevention of slope instability risks. Engineering practice verification shows that the early warning accuracy of this technical system reaches over 90%, and the survey cycle is shortened by 40%, providing comprehensive technical support for the safe operation and maintenance of HVDC transmission towers in complex terrain of Guangxi, and having important practical significance for promoting the digital and intelligent innovation of the HVDC system.

title: HD-SDNet: A Forest Fire Smoke Detection Model Based on Hierarchical Deformable Transformer
authors: Yinfang Wu, Xuanbo Xu, Xinwei Hong
abstract: Forest fires pose a serious threat to human life and property, making accurate and rapid detection of forest fire smoke crucial for fire prevention. Traditional convolutional neural network (CNN)-based smoke detection methods rely heavily on manually designed components and exhibit limited capability in detecting small or indistinct smoke in complex forest environments. To improve detection accuracy and efficiency, this study proposes an early forest fire smoke detection model based on a hierarchical deformable Transformer (HD-SDNet). The model integrates a Dilated Context Fusion and Contrastive (DCFC) module and a Hierarchical Deformable Transformer Module (HD-DETR) to enhance the perception of small or faint smoke features. Furthermore, to improve detection precision and reduce false positives and missed detections, we introduce an Iterative Bounding Box Optimization (IBBO) method that generates bounding boxes that tightly enclose smoke targets. The proposed method is validated using a custom dataset containing forest fire smoke images at various scales. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the improved model achieves significantly higher accuracy than existing state-of-the-art methods. The proposed HD-SDNet is well-suited for early forest fire smoke detection, exhibiting high detection accuracy for smoke targets across different scales.

title: Systematic Feature and Method Selection for Two-Phase Flow Classification Using Multi-Wavelength Near-Infrared Sensors
authors: Thiago Martins, João P. Bedretchuk, Victor N. Kürschner, Raphael D. Comesanha S, Anderson W. Spengler, Jorge Luiz Goes Oliveira, Kleber Vieira De Paiva
abstract: Accurate classification of two-phase gas-liquid flows is essential for reliable monitoring and control in industrial systems. Although several optical and machine-learning studies have been reported, most still focus on single wavelengths or isolated feature domains, lacking an integrated methodology that links sensor physics, feature extraction, and machine learning. This study addresses this gap by proposing the Multi-Feature Multi-Domain (MFMD) framework and its derived Optimal Feature and Method Selection (OFMS) guideline, providing a unified methodology to achieve high accuracy and model parsimony. The methodology was experimentally validated in horizontal air–water flows (five patterns: bubble, plug, slug, stratified, and wavy) using collimated near-infrared (NIR) sensors operating at 850, 980, and 1310 nm, selected for their distinct absorption sensitivities to interfacial, hybrid, and volumetric characteristics. Features were extracted from the time (Probability Density Function, PDF), frequency (Power Spectral Density, PSD), and time-frequency (Discrete Wavelet Transform, DWT) domains. This multi-domain feature set was processed by comparing feature selection (Recursive Feature Elimination, RFE) with transformation methods (Linear Discriminant Analysis, LDA, and Principal Component Analysis, PCA). Classification was performed using Support Vector Machine (SVM), k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), Random Forest (RF), and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). Results demonstrate that multi-wavelength fusion is essential for reliable flow discrimination. The RFE-based approach achieved 100% accuracy when combining all data sources, while supervised LDA significantly outperformed PCA by enhancing class separability. The resulting OFMS guideline consolidates these findings into a practical tool for selecting the most effective feature–classifier strategy, whether prioritizing maximum accuracy or a more parsimonious machine learning model.

title: The Critical Role of Interfacial Materials and Boundary Conditions in the Wind-Induced Stress of Post Insulators: A Numerical Investigation
authors: Hebin Sun, Hui Li
abstract: The fracture of outdoor disconnecting switch post insulators under strong wind loads poses a severe threat to power grid safety. Traditional simplified models often neglect the effects of multi-material stiffness mismatch and idealized boundary conditions, leading to inaccurate stress predictions. This study systematically investigates the influence mechanisms of interfacial materials and boundary conditions on the bending stress distribution of post insulators through a refined finite element model that incorporates the porcelain body, cast iron flange, and cement mortar layer. Using COMSOL Multiphysics, mechanical responses under four boundary conditions and varying wind speeds were simulated, with mesh independence rigorously verified (stress variation <2% for element size <0.04 m). Results indicate that the maximum stress consistently localizes at the neck connecting the flange and porcelain body. The inclusion of the cement mortar layer increases the peak stress by approximately 1.3 times compared to models without cement, due to stiffness mismatch. Boundary conditions significantly alter constraint stiffness, with stress amplification reaching 64% under extreme cases. Neglecting these factors leads to non-conservative design evaluations. This study provides a critical theoretical basis for the precise mechanical modeling and safety-oriented design of post insulatort.

title: A Robotic Laser Welding Seam Tracking Method for Complex 3-D Weld Trajectories Based on Geometric Structure Analysis_supp2-3661009.mp4
authors: Juan Liu
abstract: Seam sampling with line-structured-light sensors in laser welding is prone to projection distortion due to the complex 3-D geometry of sheet-metal workpieces, which leads to viewpoint-dependent variations in the sampled seam profile and affects laser incidence direction planning. Moreover, stable target tracking control becomes challenging when the 3-D seam trajectory contains small curvature radii or right-angle transitions. To address these issues, a method is proposed that compensates for projection distortion via deep-learning-based local surface-normal estimation and achieves robust viewpoint control along complex 3-D seam trajectories using prior geometric approximation of the seam trajectory. For normal estimation, seam features are encoded as “point-vector pair” superpixels and extracted by a single-stage deep convolutional network, enabling efficient, robust detection under welding glare, spatter, smoke, and reflections while maintaining boundary stability. Using the “point-vector pair” representation, the laser incidence direction is adaptively adjusted with reference to estimated surface normals to reduce pose uncertainty caused by projection distortion. For viewpoint control, a feedforward leading-direction control algorithm, derived from geometric–numerical analysis of predefined trajectory, ensures stable perception and high-precision continuous tracking along tight curves and right-angle turns. Across two experiments involving different workpiece geometries, the method achieves mean absolute laser focal position errors of 0.16 and 0.20 mm and mean absolute 3-D laser incident-direction errors of 0.99<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> and 1.58<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>, validating its efficiency, stability, and accuracy for sheet-metal laser seam tracking.

title: Transforming Future Data Center Operations and Management via Physical AI
authors: Zhiwei Cao, Minghao Li, Feng Ling, Jimin Jia, Yonggang Wen, Jianxiong Yin, Simon See
abstract: Data centers (DCs) are critical for artificial intelligence (AI) and the digital economy, with AIDCs introducing new operational challenges. This research proposes a novel Physical AI (PhyAI) framework to advance DC operations and management. The system features three core modules: an industry-grade in-house DC simulation engine for high-fidelity AIDC modeling, an AI engine built on NVIDIA PhysicsNeMo for training and evaluating physics-informed machine learning (PIML) models, and a digital twin platform based on NVIDIA Omniverse. This framework enables the creation of real-time digital twins to digitalize, optimize, and automate future DC operations. A case study demonstrated its effectiveness in predicting thermal and airflow profiles for a large-scale DC in real-time, achieving a median absolute temperature prediction error of 0.18 °C, outperforming traditional CFD/HT simulations. This emerging approach would open doors to several potential research directions for advancing PhyAI in future DC operations.

title: Gauss&#x2013;Newton Temporal Difference Learning With Nonlinear Function Approximation_supp1-3661291.pdf
authors: Junyu Zhang
abstract: In this article, a Gauss–Newton temporal difference (GNTD) learning method is proposed to solve the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Q$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-learning problem with nonlinear function approximations. In each iteration, our method takes one Gauss–Newton (GN) step to optimize a variant of mean-squared Bellman error (MSBE), where target networks are adopted to avoid double sampling. Inexact GN steps are analyzed so that one can safely and efficiently compute the GN updates by cheap matrix iterations. Under mild conditions, nonasymptotic finite-sample convergence to the globally optimal <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Q$</tex-math> </inline-formula> function is derived for various nonlinear function approximations. In particular, for neural network parameterization with ReLU activation, GNTD achieves an improved sample complexity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tilde {\mathcal {O}}(\varepsilon ^{-1})$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, as opposed to the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {\mathcal {O}}(\varepsilon ^{-2})$</tex-math> </inline-formula> sample complexity of the existing neural temporal difference (TD) methods. An <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tilde {\mathcal {O}}(\varepsilon ^{-1.5})$</tex-math> </inline-formula> sample complexity of GNTD is also established for general smooth function approximations. We validate our method via extensive experiments on several reinforcement learning (RL) benchmarks, where GNTD exhibits both higher rewards and faster convergence than TD-type methods.

title: Driver Maneuver Recognition through Semantic Representation and Spatiotemporal Structural Information
authors: Bin He, Ningmei Yu, Hongcheng Han
abstract: In intelligent transportation systems, consumer electronics like in-vehicle cameras and servers will play a vital role in real-time driver behavior recognition, enhancing both decision-making and system responsiveness. Human-machine shared driving, a core component of future systems, requires precise understanding of driver behavior and intent to support vehicle decisions and real-time traffic control, ensuring networked vehicular safety. The task of driver behavior recognition faces three key challenges: limited data leading to poor model generalization, conventional transfer learning lacking task-specific semantic understanding, and difficulty in capturing fine-grained spatiotemporal behavioral dynamics. To address these, we propose a text-driven driver maneuver recognition approach that combines the semantic analysis capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with the spatiotemporal modeling strength of video Variational Autoencoders (VAEs). Utilizing the CLIP text encoder, our method integrates environmental semantics from both in-car and external settings, capturing specific targets and their movement relationships with greater clarity. Furthermore, Open-SORA’s VAE encoder provides structured video information, enhancing the model’s comprehension of latent video space, and advancing recognition accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, offering an efficient and robust solution for intelligent transportation. The proposed model supports complex driving scenarios, marking significant progress toward safer, responsive, and intelligent driving systems.
