title: Semi-Supervised Building Change Detection From Bi-Temporal Remote Sensing Images Leveraging Visual-Language Models and Consistency Learning
authors: Wei Liu, Jianglin He, Yuhang Zhong, Yongtao Yu, Zhiming Luo, Haiyan Guan, Jonathan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Building change detection from bi-temporal remote sensing images aims to identify newly constructed, demolished, or modified buildings by comparing observations acquired at different times. A major limitation of existing supervised approaches is their strong dependence on pixel-wise building and change annotations, which are costly and labor-intensive to obtain at scale. To address this challenge, we propose SemiBCD, a semi-supervised framework that integrates visual–language model (VLM) priors with consistency learning for building change detection under limited supervision. A VLM pre-trained on natural images is first employed to generate building pseudo-labels for remote sensing imagery. To mitigate domain shift and suppress noise in these pseudo-labels, we introduce an Uncertainty-Aware Pseudo-Label Refinement (UAPLR) module that progressively improves pseudo-label reliability during training. Leveraging a small set of annotated change masks together with the refined pseudo-labels, we further design a dual consistency learning strategy, combining perturbation-based multi-view consistency and temporal-hierarchical alignment to better exploit unlabeled data. Experiments on the LEVIR-CD and WHU-CD benchmarks, as well as the WHU Building dataset, demonstrate that SemiBCD consistently outperforms representative semi-supervised baselines under low-label regimes. The results indicate that incorporating VLM priors with uncertainty-aware refinement and consistency regularization provides an effective solution for reducing annotation dependency in building change detection. The source code will be released upon publication at https://github.com/ hejianglin2001/SemiBCD.git.

title: Orthogonal Projected Gradient Differential Neural Solution to Linear and Quadratic Constrained Optimization: Theory and Applications
authors: Ying Liufu, Xin Lv, Long Jin, Shuai Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: In this research, an orthogonal projected gradient differential neural solution (OPGDNS) is introduced, specifically tailored for addressing linear and quadratic constrained optimization (LQCO) problems. The orthogonal projection theorem and gradient information are strategically leveraged, enabling the proposed solution to exhibit superior efficacy over existing methods, particularly those derived from a differential neural solution (DNS) standpoint, in handling linear and quadratic constraints. Theorems and proofs concerning the stability and convergence of the proposed OPGDNS model for LQCO problems are established. Finally, a numerical example is given, and experiments on the robust adaptive beamforming and the redundant robot are implemented, substantiating the applicability and advantage of the proposed OPGDNS model.

title: HRUnet: a deep learning model designed for lightning forecasting at high spatial resolution
authors: Jiahao Wu, Rubin Jiang, Zhuling Sun, Zhaowu Liu, Xiushu Qie, Hongbo Zhang, Shanqing Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Lightning constitutes a significant threat to human life and property. Effective lightning forecasting significantly mitigates associated losses. However, a critical gap persists in the lack of methods capable of providing precise lightning forecasts at high spatial resolution (1km×1km). To address this limitation, we propose High-Resolution U-Net (HRUnet), a novel deep learning model integrating the U-Net encoder-decoder architecture with IR-CBAM block for temporal feature enhancement and PixelShuffle for spatial resolution preservation. Furthermore, we introduce a specialized Focus Loss function to alleviate the severe class imbalance inherent in lightning prediction tasks. HRUnet was evaluated using weather radar and lightning data from Binzhou, Shandong Province, China. Experimental results demonstrate that: (1) The IR-CBAM module, PixelShuffle module, and Focus Loss function yield improvements of 7.22%, 0.26%, and 4.02% respectively in average ETS compared to the baseline U-Net; collectively, HRUnet achieves a 10.86% absolute ETS improvement. (2) On the test set, HRUnet attains performance metrics of 0.9843 hit rate, 0.6015 probability of detection, 0.2856 false alarm rate, and 0.4713 ETS. HRUnet outperforms the other four state-of-the-art deep learning models and demonstrates the best capability in forecasting the initiation, development, and dissipation of lightning clusters among them.

title: Sum-Rate Maximization for FRIS-enabled NOMA Communications
authors: Mohd Hamza Naim Shaikh, Kai-Kit Wong, Farshad Rostami Ghadi, Chan-Byoung Chae, Yangyang Zhang
abstract: The fluid antenna system (FAS) concept introduces shape- and position-reconfigurability, unlocking significant performance gains for future wireless networks. In this letter, we extend it to fluid reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (FRIS), where each reflecting element can reposition itself on demand within a prescribed subregion. We investigate a multi-user FRIS-assisted downlink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) system and formulate a network-level sum-rate maximization problem that jointly involves user clustering, FRIS element selection, phase-shift design, and NOMA power allocation under per-user quality-of-service (QoS) constraints. The resulting mixed-integer non-linear program is computationally challenging due to its hybrid binary-continuous decision space and the spatial correlation across the FRIS surface. To address this, we adopt a two-stage design: (i) distance-based interleaved clustering that partitions the U users into C NOMA clusters scheduled in orthogonal time slots, and (ii) per-cluster FRIS-NOMA optimization. For each cluster, we first apply a cross-entropy (CE) scheme to select a high-quality FRIS element-activation mask using a lightweight multi-user cascaded channel-gain metric, and then optimize the continuous phase shifts and NOMA power coefficients for the selected mask via a grey wolf optimizer (GWO) with a penalty-augmented fitness function. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed FRIS-NOMA framework yields substantial sum-rate gains over conventional RIS-aided NOMA/OMA benchmarks and operates close to an ideal continuous-phase FRIS upper bound across diverse propagation conditions.

title: Fluidic Camming for Grasping_supp2-3668055.mp4
authors: Anders Seawright
abstract: Soft robotic grippers offer certain advantages for grasping objects and anchoring into environments compared to traditional rigid grippers. For instance, their conformability enables handling of delicate objects with simplified sensing and control, and their compliance allows them to be robust to impacts. However, soft grippers generally face two significant challenges: (i) an inability to apply large grasping forces, and (ii) the use of large and cumbersome pumps and power supplies, particularly for pneumatic systems. Here, we propose “fluidic camming,” in which the force pulling on a gripper device pressurizes its actuating fluid, which, in turn, applies pressure to the grasped surface. The larger the pulling force is, the larger the grasping force automatically becomes. Fluidic camming results in the ability to grasp with very high squeezing forces. The presented devices are lightweight, work across a large range of feature shapes and sizes, perform both negative and positive-space grasping, and do not require pumps, batteries, or disposable canisters to generate system pressure. This technology is especially interesting for anchoring robots or humans to negative spaces in environments, such as cavities on a rock face, or as a gripper for gently manipulating a wide variety of both heavy and fragile objects. A device with mass 55 g anchored 667 N to a parallel-walled cavity; another device, capable of negative and positive feature grasping, successfully lifted both a chicken egg and a 222 N weight.

title: Cyber Physical Systems Security: Bridging Privacy, Verification, Intelligence, and Forensics
authors: K Ramya, Sutirth Rath, P.B. Venkataraman, Ratnesh Kumar
abstract: Cyber Physical Systems (CPS) now underpin critical sectors including energy, healthcare, transportation, and industrial automation, yet their tight coupling of computational intelligence with physical processes exposes them to sophisticated, multi-layer attacks. This survey provides a unified, end-to-end examination of CPS security by consolidating advances in privacy-preserving computation, hardware-rooted trust, intelligent intrusion detection, formal assurance, and digital forensics. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first survey to holistically interconnect these pillars within a comprehensive CPS security framework. The review introduces a structured taxonomy linking CPS security attributes to system assets, attack vectors, and defence strategies, and surveys publicly available CPS datasets, benchmarking environments, and experimental testbeds that support reproducible evaluation. It traces the evolution of differential privacy for data protection, physically unclonable functions for device identity, blockchain for integrity and provenance, AI/ML for adaptive threat detection, and formal verification for correctness and safety, while highlighting the emerging role of forensic readiness. Despite notable progress, CPS security faces persistent challenges in scalability, adversarial robustness, real-time deployment, interoperability, and benchmarking. To address these gaps, the article outlines a research roadmap emphasizing hybrid defence architectures, standardized datasets and testbeds, benchmarking standards, and adaptive governance models for resilient CPS ecosystems.

title: Eliminating polarization noises in a pulse-coded Brillouin fiber sensor via periodic polarization scrambling
authors: Zonglei Li, Gai Ying, Yin Zhou, Jia Ye, Xihua Zou, Wei Pan, Lianshan Yan
abstract: Gain-related polarization noise, induced by the use of random polarization scrambling (PS) for the purpose of eliminating polarization fading, is a main performance-limiting issue for a current pulse-coded Brillouin optical time-domain analysis (BOTDA) fiber sensor. Here, a periodic PS approach based on differ-frequency acousto-optic modulation is proposed to reduce polarization noises. It not only permits enough polarization states for polarization fading suppression, but also considerably reduces the polarization randomness between the pump and probe at any fiber position for polarization noise reduction compared to the commonly used random PS approach. This enhances the signal-to-noise ratio of Brillouin gain by ~ 4 dB, resulting in the enhancement of the measurement accuracy by a factor of 2.5 at the far fiber end. Our work reveals that periodic PS offers new possibilities for mitigating polarization-related impairments in Brillouin fiber sensors since it enables moderate polarization randomness.

title: Tracking and Aiming System Based on Laser Dual-Pulse Interval Modulation
authors: Sida Li, Huihui Pan, Min Tao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Target tracking technology is widely applied in fields such as weapon guidance. This paper proposes a target tracking and aiming method that integrates optical communication with beam steering. Unlike traditional schemes relying on the energy distribution of laser spots for angle measurement, this method encodes target position information into pulse sequences using Dual-Pulse Interval Modulation (DPIM). The position information is recovered at the tracking end via interval demodulation of the target's reflected echo to achieve closed-loop pointing control. This time-domain decision mechanism is inherently insensitive to multipath-induced amplitude fluctuations and energy dispersion, provided pulses are detectable. The system utilizes a two-axis mechanical galvanometer and a Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) to implement DPIM laser pointing control, as well as modulation and demodulation. Experimental results demonstrate that, in indoor prototype tracking tests, the pointing and tracking Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< 0.028^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Comparative experiments further verify the robustness of the method under speckle and energy dispersion conditions. Collectively, these results demonstrate a feasible architecture for the integration of laser communication and laser tracking.

title: NCRAgent: A Neuro-Inspired Multiagent Framework for Transparent and Explainable Medical VQA
authors: Sirui Liu, Keyu Hou, Ali Anaissi, Ali Braytee
abstract: Medical Visual Question Answering (MedVQA) systems are increasingly important for helping clinicians interpret and diagnose medical images. However, many existing MedVQA solutions provide limited support for ambiguous queries and lack sufficient clarity in how reports are generated, weakening their usefulness for clinical decision-making. To address these issues, we propose a multi-agent system inspired by human neurological processes to improve accuracy, transparency, and explainability. Our framework includes three specialized agents: (1) a Query Rewrite Agent (inspired by the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex) that decomposes complex queries into structured sub-questions, (2) a Visual Analysis Agent (modeled after the inferotemporal cortex) that interprets medical images, and (3) a Report Generation Agent (drawing on the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex) that synthesizes final answers. The system exposes its internal reasoning steps by dividing tasks across multiple agents, ensuring a transparent decision-making process. Specifically, we break down the original query into smaller, interactive sub-questions. On the other hand, our report generation process incorporates positive and negative keywords, further enhancing the explainability of the final output. Related code and prompts will be available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lv3mmray/NCRAgent</uri>.

title: Fuzzy Measure-Guided Semi-Supervised Breast Cancer Image Segmentation Network
authors: Ran Zhang, Kaihong Guo
abstract: Histopathological breast cancer images often suffer from structural heterogeneity and unclear or complex boundaries. Acquiring pixel-level annotations is costly, limiting the effectiveness and generalizability of traditional segmentation methods. To address these challenges, we propose the Fuzzy Measure-Guided Semi-Supervised Breast Cancer Image Segmentation Network (FuzMGNet). This approach combines fuzzy measures with Convolutional Recurrent Neural Networks (CRNN) and employs Choquet integral-based non-additive feature fusion. A pseudo-labeling guidance mechanism is used to improve boundary delineation. FuzMGNet captures multi-scale contextual information through hierarchical convolutional encoding and spatial modeling using recurrent units. The fuzzy measure dynamically adjusts feature fusion strategies, enhancing the network's adaptability across different images. The Choquet integral strengthens the model's ability to handle complex dependencies, improving segmentation accuracy. Finally, the pseudo-labeling mechanism enables effective training with limited labeled data. Experimental results show that FuzMGNet significantly outperforms traditional deep learning segmentation methods on the MIAS, BreakHis, and BACH datasets.

title: Design and Implementation of N-Type-Only LTPS-TFT Voltage Reference Circuits for Flexible Electronics
authors: Chao Wang, Yiyang Chen, Wangzilu Lu, Jiajie Huang, Yuting Hou, Jian Zhao, Hui Wang, Chen Jiang, Yongfu Li
abstract: This paper details the design and simulation-based evaluation of three distinct N-type-only low-temperature polycrystalline silicon thin-film transistor (LTPS-TFT) voltage reference circuits, tailored for the specific demands of flexible electronic systems. Addressing the inherent design challenges in TFT technology, these circuits employ a common strategy: generating a current with a negative temperature coefficient to compensate for proportional-to-absolute-temperature (PTAT) voltage characteristics. Comprehensive post-layout and statistical Monte Carlo simulation results highlight that the first of the proposed configurations achieves superior performance, exhibiting a temperature coefficient (TC) of 34.03 ppm/°C, a line sensitivity of 0.61 %/V, and a power supply rejection ratio (PSRR) of -42.34 dB. This study presents new circuit-level approaches for achieving reliable voltage references in flexible applications, thereby advancing the development of high-performance, flexible electronic systems.

title: Miniaturized Bandpass Filters Using MISL-Based Slow Wave Meander Line Resonators
authors: Hui Liu, Yi Wu, Kaixue Ma, Shixiong Liang, Sheng Li
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a miniaturized wideband bandpass filter (BPF) with flexible transmission zeros (TZs) using slow wave meander line resonators (SWMLRs) based on metal integrated suspended line (MISL) technology. The TZ positions of the BPF are flexibly controllable via different SWMLR arrangements. A third-order BPF is designed and fabricated by introducing separate electric and magnetic coupling paths (SEMCPs) and source-load coupling, enabling multiple TZs on both sides of the passband. Benefiting from the all-metal MISL structure (copper sheets), the BPF eliminates dielectric loss, achieving a low insertion loss (IL) of 0.28 dB. The filter size is as compact as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.11\lambda _{g} \times 0.092\lambda _{g} \times 0.067\lambda _{g}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Finally, the measured results were in good agreement with the simulation results, verifying the proposed design as a promising solution for miniaturized high-performance microwave filters.

title: Fast Reliability Evaluation of Distribution Network Based on Graph Neural Network
authors: Liang Ma, Jinshan Luo, Shigong Jiang, Xiaohan Li, Chenyi Si
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: With the increasing demand for distribution network planning, rapid and accurate evaluation of the reliability of planning schemes can reduce the evaluation time and provide a basis for breakthroughs in distribution network planning technology. This paper mainly uses graph neural networks(GNN) to achieve rapid assessment of reliability. By representing the results of distribution network planning as a set of graph, topology structure is considered as one of the factors affecting reliability. Through the testing cases of medium-voltage and highvoltage, it can be seen that compared with traditional simulation based Monte Carlo methods, the evaluation error of using graph neural networks is smaller, and the evaluation time is greatly shortened, which points out the direction for future research on reliability evaluation methods.

title: Analysis and Optimization of Abnormal Shutdown Timing in Kunliulong Three-Terminal Hybrid DC Operation with Minimum Power
authors: Chang Ruan, Wanli Shi, Hangtian Dong, Yukun Xiao, Xiaoxia Li, Sai Ding, Peng Zhang, Jianhua Lu
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: The Kunliulong DC project is the world's first ± <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 0 0 k V}$</tex> UHV large-capacity multi-terminal hybrid flexible DC project. The project consists of three converter stations and highvoltage transmission lines connecting each station. It has more flexible system adjustment capabilities than the traditional twoterminal DC system, but it also increases the complexity of the control and protection strategy. Operation abnormalities have occurred many times since it was put into operation, and the reliability of the DC control and protection system and equipment needs to be further improved. In view of abnormal outage timing events under Kunliulong three-terminal hybrid DC minimum power operation, this paper comprehensively combs the multi-terminal DC coordinated control principle and response situation, and proposes an extremely controlled minimum power operation lower limit update logic timing optimization strategy to improve the outage process. This strategy successfully eliminated the short-term power rise phenomenon, improved the coordinated control capability of the DC control and protection system in the minimum power operation mode, and effectively improved the reliability and stability of the Kunliulong three-terminal hybrid DC project operation. The research results can provide reference and guidance for the subsequent UHV multi-terminal hybrid DC system control and protection function design.

title: Nonzero-Sum Differential Game-Based Distributed Optimal Control of Reconfigurable Robot with Physical Human-Robot Interaction
authors: Bing Ma, Zebin Ji, Xinye Zhu, Tianjiao An
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: A reconfigurable robot (RR) designed for humanrobot collaborative task is investigated, wherein a distributed optimal control scheme based on nonzero-sum game theory is proposed. A dynamic model of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R R}$</tex> is established using joint torque feedback technology. By leveraging differential game theory, the optimal control problem of the RR system under physical human-robot interaction is transformed into a nonzero-sum game problem involving multiple robot subsystems. To solve the coupled Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) equations, an adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) algorithm is employed, integrating a novel nonzero-sum game approach, thereby formulating a distributed optimal control strategy for human-robot collaboration tasks. Experimental results are provided to validate the superiority and effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Entity Typing on Hyper-Relational Knowledge Graphs via Instance-Ontology Contrastive Learning
authors: Cong Wu, Congcong Tian, Yu Cao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: Compared to triplets, hyper-relational facts include a main triplet along with key-value pairs. This structure allows knowledge graph embedding techniques to model complex relationships more effectively and improves feature representation. Some approaches use ontology as constraints or learn from both hyper-relational facts and ontology. However, they often overlook ontology hierarchy. They also struggle with factual data dominating the ontology, which results in suboptimal performance. To address these issues, we propose a model that fuses instance data with ontology knowledge for collaborative learning. The model constructs separate embedding spaces for instances and ontologies. It employs multi-granularity feature extraction within each view. A cross-view contrastive learning mechanism then aligns and integrates semantic information. Experimental results show that this approach performs better than existing methods on the entity typing task. This confirms the benefit of combining instance and ontological knowledge with contrastive learning.

title: Effect of Winding Placement in Magnetoelastic Sensors Using a Prototype Setup for Bending Force Sensor Testing
authors: Šimon Gans, Ján Molnár, Mariia Maliakova, Anna Iudina
publish: 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Modern Electrical and Energy System (MEES)
abstract: In this paper 4 magnetoelastic sensors with different winding placements are tested. Testing sensors to static loadings can be a time-consuming process and therefore a prototype equipment is described which allows for a semi-automated measurement process. A control application was developed that collects and visualizes data from the measurement. The applied load is measured by a resistive load cell and compared to the sensor being evaluated. Four sensor samples are tested. Their sensitivity, linearity, and hysteresis error are computed automatically. Winding placement on the sensors is tested as a factor affecting sensitivity.

title: Small-Signal Stability Analysis of PV-Penetrated SMIB Systems Considering Control Measurement Delays
authors: Aditia Dermawan, Husni Rois Ali, Avrin Nur Widiastuti, Naval Rudiyanto
publish: 2025 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence, Data Engineering and Sciences (ICAIDES)
abstract: High penetration of photovoltaic (PV) generation introduces new stability challenges due to reduced mechanical inertia and increased dependence on inverter control dynamics. This paper examines the combined impact of $\mathbf{P V}$ penetration and control-loop time delays on the small-signal stability of a Single Machine Infinite Bus (SMIB) system employing a grid-following inverter. Modal analysis is used to track eigenvalue movement and damping ratio variations for PV penetration levels ranging from 0% to 90%. In parallel, time-domain simulations are performed to assess transient responses of rotor angle, rotor speed, and active power following a $\mathbf{1 0 0 ~ m s}$ three-phase fault. The results show that increasing PV penetration consistently decreases system damping, shifting the critical electromechanical modes closer to the imaginary axis and reducing the damping ratio from $\mathbf{5. 0 0 \%}$ to $\mathbf{2. 6 8 \%}$. Furthermore, measurement delays in the inverter control loop particularly around 500 ms significantly degrade the dynamic response, causing prolonged oscillations and slower post-fault recovery. These findings demonstrate that the combination of high PV penetration and control latency poses fundamental stability concerns and highlight the need for improved inverter control strategies to ensure reliable operation of PV-dominated power systems.

title: Physics-Informed Graph Neural Network for Photovoltaic Power Forecasting in Low-Voltage DC Systems
authors: Naiqing Li, Minglei Xie, Liang Zou, Zhiyun Han
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: As the penetration rate of photovoltaic power generation continues to increase in DC transmission and distribution systems, its randomness and volatility pose severe challenges to the stable operation of DC systems. Achieving high-precision, high-reliability PV power forecasting is a key prerequisite for enhancing the intelligence level of DC systems. However, existing data-driven forecasting models suffer from poor physical consistency and weak interpretability, limiting their practical application in engineering. Therefore, this paper proposes a physics-guided spatio-temporal graph convolutional network (PIGCN) model. This model constructs spatiotemporal coupling relationships among regional <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P V}$</tex> power plants through a graph structure and embeds the physical equations of PV power generation as soft constraints within the neural network training process. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PIGCN model achieves significantly improved prediction accuracy compared to traditional data-driven models while ensuring that the prediction results comply with physical laws.

title: TSN-based Multi-service Traffic Scheduling Algorithm
authors: Weijian Zhang, Li Di, Mengxue Zhou, Haibin Zhao, Shuqi Wang, Rui Gong, Xuanzhong Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing and Communication Technology (SPCT)
abstract: To address the issues of balancing delay and resource allocation in traffic scheduling under high-load and multi-service-stream concurrent conditions within smart substations, we propose a traffic scheduling algorithm that combines a hierarchical mapping mechanism with the GCN-TD3 algorithm. The mapping mechanism differentiates services based on criticality, while the GCN-TD3 algorithm enables adaptive, dynamic resource scheduling. Experimental results demonstrate that under conditions of concurrent multi-service flows and high load, the proposed method enhances the scheduling success rate and throughput while optimizing latency and resource allocation fairness.

title: Improving Usability Augmented Reality With Code Complexity and Maintainability Index
authors: Hendriyana, Haryono Soeparno, Yulyani Arifin, Ford Lumban Gaol
publish: 2025 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence, Data Engineering and Sciences (ICAIDES)
abstract: Education is a crucial role in improving students skills and competencies. Therefore, learning resources are needed that support and align with Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 4: quality education. The fundamental areas of computer science is ability to recognize computer architecture and hardware, which allows implementation of immersive learning based Android called Kenal Hardware. However, the evaluation results of this application showed low interface usability (42.86%) and a high system error rate (24.49%). Consequently, this study measured software quality are internal and external quality. External quality is measured through direct user experience, while internal quality is measured using internal parameters such as code, code complexity, lines of code, and others. This study focused on code structure, using Cyclometric Complexity (CC) to calculate complexity, while the Maintainability Index (MI) is an indicator of the software’s maintainability. The research consisted of three stages: prefactoring, matrix refactoring, and revalidation. The result of internal quality improvements reduced error rate M1 (Marker Detection) 54.5% and in M2 (Interaction 3D Objects) 22.1%. Furthermore, improvements in M3 (Main Menu and User Interface) resulted in significant enhancements, which were implemented in new version of Kenal hardware.

title: Scalability via Sparsity in Stackelberg Security Games: An Augmented Decision Space Approach
authors: Adam Żychowski, Abhishek Gupta, Yew-Soon Ong, Jacek Mańdziuk
publish: ACM Transactions on Evolutionary Learning and Optimization
abstract: None

title: Semantic-Structure Enhanced Knowledge Graph Completion with Hard Negative Sampling
authors: Zhiqiang Ding, Jingli Zhao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: Knowledge graph completion aims to infer missing triples from known entities, but real-world knowledge graphs are typically incomplete. While existing text-based methods can leverage entity descriptions, they still suffer from inefficient negative sampling and over-clustering issues in loss function design. To address these challenges, this paper proposes SSEKGC (Semantic-Structure Enhanced Knowledge Graph Completion), a method that integrates semantic and structural information for knowledge graph completion. Specifically, we propose a Top-K hard negative sampling strategy that dynamically selects the K most similar entities to positive samples as negative samples, enabling the model to continuously face the most challenging decision boundaries. We design a Top-K-based triplet loss function that optimizes only hard negative samples, combined with a margin mechanism to avoid excessive separation, significantly reducing computational complexity while mitigating over-clustering and under-clustering issues. Experiments on benchmark datasets including WN18RR and FB15k-237 demonstrate that SSEKGC outperforms the current state-of-the-art method SimKGC by 5.97 and 3.56 percentage points in MRR, respectively, achieving optimal performance across all evaluation metrics.

title: An Adaptive Enhanced PCC-FCM Method for Anomalous Load Data Imputation
authors: Ying Weng, Yulin Chen, Donglian Qi, Xing Huang, Li Li, Xudong Ouyang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power System and Renewable Energy (PSRE)
abstract: With the increasing intelligence of power systems, load data plays a crucial role in system scheduling and forecasting. However, due to equipment failures and external disturbances, load data is prone to anomalies, which can significantly affect the accuracy and stability of system operations. To address this issue, this paper proposes a load data imputation method for anomalies based on an adaptively improved Pearson Correlation Coefficient-Fuzzy C-Means (PCC-FCM) clustering algorithm. The method incorporates PCC to enhance the distance metric of the FCM algorithm, enabling a dynamic daily load curve clustering approach that improves the precision of user load behavior feature extraction. Then, the Snake Optimization (SO) algorithm is employed to adaptively optimize the number of clusters, further enhancing clustering accuracy. Based on the extracted feature curves from each cluster, a curve substitution method is used to more accurately impute anomalous load data. Finally, experimental validation using U.S. DOE Open Energy Information datasets demonstrates that the proposed method achieves superior imputation accuracy compared to conventional approaches.

title: Hierarchical Reasoning and LoRa-Enhanced Large Model Framework for Root Cause Analysis in Distributed Systems
authors: Kangning Gao, Luyao Men, Zijun Zhou, Erdi Gao, Xinyue Huang, Junyi Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: Root cause analysis in distributed systems is difficult because of the large amount of observability data, complex service dependencies, and the need for fast diagnosis. Traditional graph or statistical methods have low efficiency and poor generalization when they handle high-dimensional data. This paper presents QwenRCA, a hierarchical large-model framework built on the Qwen language model. QwenRCA uses temporal fault summarization, low-rank domain adaptation, structured reasoning, confidence-based routing, and safe automated repair generation. The model compresses multi-modal data into short representations, adjusts parameters through LoRA for domain learning, and routes inference between models of different sizes based on task difficulty and confidence. By combining simple reasoning and controlled automation, QwenRCA provides an efficient and interpretable solution for real-time fault localization and repair in distributed environments.

title: Research on Coordinated Current Limiting Functionality for Three Substations in the Yunnan-Guizhou Interconnection Project
authors: Zicong Zhang, Xinwen Wu, Qiyao Shen, Shiwei Zhao, Jialei Wang, Jianxiang Huang
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: The Yunnan-Guizhou Interconnection Project is the world's first <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \text{5 0 0 k V}$</tex> HVDC (High-Voltage Direct Current) transmission project converted from a two-terminal to a three-terminal system. A new <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \text{5 0 0 k V}$</tex> Luquan Converter Station is built in Luquan, Yunnan, and a new <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \text{5 0 0 k V}$</tex> DC line is constructed to connect to the completed Guizhou side of the Gaozhao DC system. This forms a three-terminal HVDC transmission project spanning Yunnan, Guizhou, and Guangdong provinces, establishing a comprehensive power transmission complex for power evacuation from Yunnan and Guizhou.In terms of the current limiting function, the three-terminal operation mode differs from that of conventional two-terminal HVDC transmission projects. This function is consistent with the power coordination principle, describing the sequence of current limiting and the calculation method of current limiting values under various fault conditions during the operation of the three stations. It defines the priority of power loss when operational faults occur in the three-station system and limits the current to an effective safe range.

title: Improving the Performance of Naïve Bayes Using Stacking Technique for Detecting Stress in Students in Indonesia
authors: M. Khairul Anam, Torkis Nasution, Helda Yenni, Mutiana Pratiwi, Ulya Ilhami Arsyah, Irwanda Syahputra
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Information System and Information Technology (ICISIT)
abstract: Early detection of stress levels among students plays a crucial role in maintaining mental health and supporting academic performance. This study aims to improve the performance of the Naïve Bayes algorithm by applying the stacking technique for detecting student stress levels in Indonesia. The dataset was obtained from the Depression Anxiety Stress Scale (DASS) questionnaire and was processed through several stages, including labeling, preprocessing, and class balancing using the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). Preprocessing ensured that the data were clean, consistent, and ready for modeling, while SMOTE addressed class imbalance to improve fairness across categories. Three variants of Naïve Bayes, namely Bernoulli, Gaussian, and Multinomial, were employed as both base models and meta models within the stacking framework. The results of baseline testing indicated that Bernoulli Naïve Bayes and Multinomial Naïve Bayes achieved an accuracy of 94%, while Gaussian Naïve Bayes obtained 93%. In the stacking phase, the Gaussian Naïve Bayes meta learner achieved the highest performance with 95% accuracy, followed by Multinomial Naïve Bayes with 94% and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes with 86%. These results demonstrate that stacking with a Gaussian Naïve Bayes meta learner can enhance accuracy and prediction stability, particularly for severe stress categories that are critical for early intervention.

title: Translating Requirements into CARLA Executable Scripts: An LLM-Driven Automated Scenario Realization
authors: Shiyang Guan, Yijun Lu, Jati H. Husen, Haowei Cheng, Hironori Washizaki, Naoyasu Ubayashi, Nobukazu Yoshioka
abstract: Scenario-based testing is critical for ensuring the safety and robustness of autonomous driving (AD) systems, particularly in extreme scenarios such as heavy rain, pedestrian-involved crashes, and nighttime conditions. Our previous work integrated the CARLA simulator with the Multi-view Modeling Framework for ML Systems (M3S), facilitating scenario generation but still requiring substantial manual scripting. In this paper, we extend our framework by incorporating Large Language Models (LLMs) with M3S to automate scenario generation. Using a hierarchical prompt design, our approach extracts structured parameters from M3S descriptions into a JSON schema, which guides the LLM to generate accurate CARLA simulation scripts. Our evaluation demonstrates significant improvements in automation accuracy and efficiency, substantially reducing manual intervention and enhancing continuous testing cycles for AD systems.

title: TimesNet-Based High Impedance Fault Detection Method in Distribution Networks
authors: Chu Tong, Mou-Fa Guo, Jian-Hong Gao, Cui Hong
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power System and Renewable Energy (PSRE)
abstract: Existing high-impedance fault (HIF) detection methods suffer from low detection accuracy due to the insufficient utilization of the non-periodicity and long duration characteristics of HIF waveforms. In this paper, we propose a TimesNet-based HIF detection method in distribution networks. TimesNet decouples the time sequence, allowing for a focus on both periodic and non-periodic waveforms, thereby addressing the issue of underutilized non-periodic features in existing methods. Once a fault was triggered by triggering algorithm, the zero-sequence current in a certain length would be captured and traversed by a sliding window. With the movement of the sliding window, the overlapped waveform would be decoupled by TimesNet into different cycles, with attention paid to the temporal variations both weekly and within cycles to achieve the HIF detection at the level of sliding window. When the obtained zero-sequence current is processed using a sliding window, the corresponding detection results at the sliding window level serve as the basis for the HIF detection results at the event level. The proportion of detection results at the sliding window level is calculated and compared with a threshold to determine whether the event as a whole constitutes a HIF. Simulation and experimental validation demonstrate that the proposed meth-od exhibits effectiveness and reliability in detecting HIF.

title: Low-Cost IoT Sensor Networks for Real-Time Water Quality Monitoring in Refugee Camps
authors: Wai Yie Leong
abstract: The need for safe drinking water in refugee camps has underscored the importance of deploying sustainable, low-cost water quality monitoring solutions. This study explores the design, implementation, and performance of a Low-Cost IoT Sensor Network (LCISN) tailored for realtime water quality monitoring in humanitarian settings. The proposed system integrates low-power microcontrollers, opensource software platforms, and a suite of sensors (pH, turbidity, temperature, and electrical conductivity) to collect and transmit water quality data over wireless mesh networks. Data are aggregated on cloud-based dashboards to enable rapid detection of contamination and facilitate timely intervention by camp management. The system emphasizes energy efficiency through solar-powered nodes and intermittent data transmission protocols, enabling extended operation in infrastructure-limited environments. The solution provides a transformative approach for water safety in crisis zones, empowering communities with actionable environmental intelligence. This research contributes to the advancement of humanitarian engineering in water quality management for displaced populations.

title: Study on Non-Uniform Pollution Deposition Characteristics and Electric Field Distortion of $\pm 800 \text{kV}$ UHVDC Transmission Line Insulators in Humid Mountainous
authors: Yangzhi Wei, Kuoteng Sun
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: This paper investigates the phenomenon of non-uniform pollution deposition at the ends of tension insulator strings on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 800 \text{kV}$</tex> UHVDC transmission lines in the humid mountainous areas of Guangxi, such as Hechi. Through field measurements and finite element simulation, the mechanisms of electric field distortion and associated insulation risks are revealed. A surface conductivity model was developed to analyze the influence of pollution distribution on the potential gradient. Results indicate that heavy pollution at the ends can increase the voltage borne by middle-section insulators by up to 25%, significantly reducing the insulation margin. Leakage current simulation shows a 25% increase in current density in the middle section, heightening the risk of partial discharge. Based on these findings, differentiated cleaning strategies and protection recommendations, optimized based on electric field distribution, are proposed, providing a theoretical basis for enhancing the external insulation reliability of UHVDC lines.

title: Hybrid Power System with Renewable Energy Sources, Gas Generator and Electric Vehicles for Livestock Farm
authors: Olexandr Shavolkin, Yurii Haman, Viacheslav Prus
publish: 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Modern Electrical and Energy System (MEES)
abstract: The aim is to improve the autonomous operation of a grid-connected hybrid power supply system for a livestock farm, using a photovoltaic system with a battery, a wind generator, a gas generator, and electric vehicles with a V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) charger. A proposed control mechanism prioritizes the charging of the main battery and regulates the gas generator's power based on deviations in the main battery's state of charge relative to the calculated value. This reduces the impact of deviations in renewable energy generation and load from forecasted values while maintaining the gas generator's power and the battery's state of charge within specified limits, reducing gas consumption, and maximizing the use of renewable energy. An algorithm has been developed for calculating daily schedules for setting the state of charge of the main battery, limiting the gas generator's power, and managing the power of electric vehicle batteries during the energy redistribution process. The system has been studied using computer modeling for various scenarios involving deviations in load and renewable energy generation.

title: High-Maneuverability Air-to-Air Interception Control for UAVs based on DRL with Shooting Mode
authors: Xiaoxin Li, Zhihong Liu, Guanzheng Wang, Tianyou Liu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Human-Computer Interaction and Robotics (AIHCIR)
abstract: Air-to-air interception by unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) requires aggressive acceleration toward the target to ensure effective impact. However, conventional deep reinforcement learning (DRL) controllers designed for point-reaching tasks often suffer from an over penalty issue when the agent overshoots the target, resulting in undesirable deceleration during the terminal phase. To address this problem, this paper proposes a novel shooting mode task formulation that terminates the episode when the UAV passes through the target plane, thus eliminating incentive slowdown. A neural network controller based on the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm is designed to directly map state errors to bodyrates and thrust commands. Simulation experiments demonstrate that, compared to the point-reaching mode, the proposed method effectively avoids terminal deceleration and achieves a high-speed interception at 15 m/s with a precision of 0.2 m. The results validate the method’s capability to support high-maneuverability aerial interception tasks.

title: An Accountable Access Control Scheme Using Edge Blockchain for Secure Data Sharing in Smart Grid
authors: Xiao Liu, Zhenyang Wei, Jining Chen, Zhou Liang, Gaoxiang Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: As the smart grid (SG) continues to evolve, ensuring information and communication security has become increasingly challenging. Conventional public key encryption methods are proving inadequate for data sharing within the SG due to their inefficiencies and vulnerabilities. To tackle these challenges, this paper introduces a lightweight accountable access control model, called LAACMEB, which is based on edge blockchain and utilizes elliptic curve cryptography (ECC). The proposed LAACMEB leverages ECC-based scalar multiplication rather than bilinear pairing, significantly enhancing the speed of the scheme. Furthermore, the proposed scheme introduces an accountability mechanism for various entities within the system. By incorporating a globally unique identifier during the key generation phase, the scheme effectively mitigates collusion attacks. In comparison to conventional ciphertext-policy attributebased encryption (CP-ABE) schemes utilizing ECC, the proposed LAACMEB leverages edge blockchain for storing both encrypted and decrypted data, with edge nodes handling partial decryption. The proposed LAACMEB thus ensures a faster, more secure, and accountable data sharing environment for the SG. Finally, the proposed scheme's effectiveness is validated through thorough security analysis and performance evaluation.

title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning with Differential Privacy for Robust Instruction Adaptation in Large Language Models
authors: Yulin Huang, Yaxuan Luan, Jinxu Guo, Xiangchen Song, Yuchen Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: This study addresses the issues of privacy protection and efficiency in instruction fine-tuning of large-scale language models by proposing a parameter-efficient method that integrates differential privacy noise allocation with gradient clipping in a collaborative optimization framework. The method keeps the backbone model frozen and updates parameters through a low-dimensional projection subspace, while introducing clipping and adaptive noise allocation during gradient computation. This design reduces privacy budget consumption and ensures training stability and robustness. The unified framework combines gradient constraints, noise allocation, and parameter projection, effectively mitigating performance fluctuations and privacy risks in multi-task instruction scenarios. Experiments are conducted across hyperparameter, environment, and data sensitivity dimensions. Results show that the method outperforms baseline models in accuracy, privacy budget, and parameter efficiency, and maintains stable performance under diverse and uncertain data conditions. The findings enrich the theoretical integration of differential privacy and parameter-efficient fine-tuning and demonstrate its practical adaptability in instruction tasks, providing a feasible solution for secure training in complex instruction environments.

title: Stochastic Scheduling and Learning-Based Control of Energy Storage in Active Distribution Systems
authors: Mukesh Gautam, Rakib Hossain, Juan C. Bedoya, Jing Xie, Di Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE Electrical Energy Storage Applications and Technologies Conference (EESAT)
abstract: This paper presents a modeling and control framework for energy storage in active distribution networks. A stochastic optimal dispatch problem is first formulated and solved to address various uncertainties in distributed resources, building loads, and demand flexibility, accounting for weather variability, time-dependent building occupancy, and unpredictable demand patterns. To ensure safe operation and avoid potential violations arising from network constraints, a deep reinforcement learning controller is designed to refine control actions in real-time. A Monte Carlo tree search is used to enhance the learning agent’s exploration capability under dynamic conditions. Simulation results on the IEEE 123-node test feeder demonstrate enhanced performance, achieving a 13% average increase in economic benefits and significantly reducing voltage violations compared to baseline and conventional approaches.

title: A Privacy-Preserving and Region-Aware Online Task Allocation Scheme in MCS with Adaptive Privacy Thresholding
authors: Yingying Ren, Hongjian Liu, Xiao Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: In mobile crowdsensing (MCS) online task allocation, workers typically need to report fine-grained location and sensing data, which may unintentionally expose sensitive personal information such as mobility trajectories, frequently visited places, or daily routines. These concrete privacy risks make workers increasingly inclined to prefer stronger privacy protection to reduce the risk of privacy leakage, while the platform expects workers to relax their protection levels to ensure sufficient data accuracy. This trade-off makes balancing privacy preservation and platform utility a critical challenge in practical MCS deployments. The situation becomes even more complex due to the significant heterogeneity in workers' privacy preferences and the varying sensitivity of different tasks to data accuracy. To address this dual heterogeneity, we propose a privacy-preserving and region-aware online task allocation scheme (PPOTA) in MCS with Adaptive privacy thresholding. The proposed scheme constructs an adaptive privacy threshold model that integrates region similarity and worker number prediction, enabling dynamic adjustment of privacy thresholds for each task based on the predicted worker availability, while achieving differentiated task-worker matching according to individual privacy exposure preferences. Furthermore, we introduce a Reinforcement Learning-based Bee Algorithm (RLBA), which employs a customized matrix encoding method and search strategy to allocate tasks under privacy constraints efficiently. Experimental results demonstrate that PPOTA can significantly improve the overall system utility while respecting the personalized privacy preferences of workers.

title: A Study of Implementing BGPsec with SCION
authors: Serina Hj Mohd Ali, Haji Rudy Erwan Ramlee, Suhrawardiy Sha’Adon
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Applied Computational Intelligence in Information Systems (ACIIS)
abstract: With the standardisation of BGPsec, it is still insufficient in providing secure routes and has raised various issues. The use of RPKI has seen to open more opportunities for exploitation. The paper proposed the study of SCION on BGPsec to test the applicability of using SCION architecture in the current Internet routing BGP. This study will conduct the testing by simulating a synthetic Internet topology and conduct comparative study.

title: Development and Evaluation of a Web-Based Document Processing System for Students of Cavite State University
authors: Jake R. Ersando, Jason L. Baskiñas, Lorienel R. Macabacyao, James Angelo V. Aves, Mark Philip M. Sy, Ria Clarisse M. Sy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence, Data Engineering and Sciences (ICAIDES)
abstract: This study developed a web-based system to address inefficiencies in the manual process of requesting academic documents. It responded to the need for faster and more accessible student services amid rising enrollment and administrative workload. Traditionally, document requests required in-person visits and manual handling, often causing delays and inconvenience. The main objective was to design an efficient and user-friendly online platform that streamlines document transactions between students and the College of Engineering and Information Technology (CEIT) Registrar’s Office. The system was developed using Laravel 9 in Visual Studio Code and tested with Selenium Integrated Development Environment (IDE) and Selenium Side Runner. Using a developmental research design, evaluations were conducted with $\mathbf{1 0}$ IT experts and $\mathbf{1 0 0}$ CEIT students, along with one Registrar staff member, through survey instruments and descriptive statistical analysis. Results indicated high satisfaction in functionality, usability, and reliability, with both technical and non-technical evaluations rating the system as excellent. Findings show that the system improves efficiency, reduces processing time, and enhances accessibility of services for students. The study concludes that web-based automation can modernize registrar operations and recommends future integration with campuswide student information systems for broader institutional benefit.

title: Nondestructive Testing of Epoxy Resin Subjected to Multi-Factor Aging using Terahertz Waves
authors: Haibin Zhou, Zhicheng Xie, Jun Deng, Xunjun He, Mingze Zhang, Yu Feng, Minghe Chi, Dong Yue
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: Epoxy resin has become a significant insulating material owing to its outstanding electrical insulation properties, high dielectric strength, and superior arc resistance, finding extensive application in high-voltage electrical equipment. However, under long-term service conditions, epoxy resin may undergo material degradation, leading to a decline in insulation performance and potentially compromising the operational stability of electrical systems. To address this challenge, effective detection of epoxy resin condition is essential. Here, we innovatively propose a nondestructive testing method based on terahertz (THz) waves for epoxy resin. By emitting THz waves into epoxy resins subjected to various aging conditions and detecting the multiple electromagnetic parameters of the transmitted waves, effective assessment and inspection of the epoxy resin can be achieved. Therefore, the proposed novel THz wave based nondestructive testing method holds significant promise for performance assessment and condition monitoring of high-voltage electrical equipment and advanced electromagnetic devices.

title: Development of a Multi-Disease Diagnostic System Utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): A Systematic Review of Deep Learning Applications in Image-Based Medical Diagnosis
authors: Halomoan Edy Manurung, Fikri Budiman, Heru Agus Santoso, Aris Marjuni
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Information System and Information Technology (ICISIT)
abstract: CNN-based multi-disease diagnosis systems enhance medical imaging, enabling detection of up to 15 disorders per image. This systematic review (52 papers, 2021–2025) analyzes trends, deficiencies, and clinical impact. Meta-analysis yielded a pooled AUC-ROC of 0.91 ± 0.04 (95% CI 0.87–0.95), with moderate heterogeneity (I<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=58%). Thematic analysis revealed five themes: multi-disease classification (29%), data imbalance (23%), hybrid models (19%), XAI (15%), and computational optimization (14%). Advantages include high accuracy (96.5%) and reduced latency (58%). Disadvantages: geographical bias (68% from China), inadequate preprocessing (28% using CLAHE), and limited interpretability (32% using SHAP/LIME). This study uniquely integrates bias, meta-analysis, and ethics. Recommendations include standardized preprocessing, diverse datasets, and comprehensive XAI for universal health access (SDG 3).

title: Differentially Private Algorithm for Quantile Regression
authors: Jun Luo, Guang Zhu, Shijie Xu, Jisheng Tong
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: Quantile regression models offer a comprehensive and robust perspective on the conditional distribution of the dependent variable and are insensitive to outliers. However, existing research has not adequately addressed data privacy protection in these models. Differential privacy, an emerging technique, provides a mathematically rigorous privacy guarantee that can resist various background knowledge attacks and precisely quantify the degree of privacy preservation. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a differential privacy algorithm for quantile regression. Theoretical analysis and experiments show that the algorithm satisfies the differential privacy definition while achieving estimation accuracy comparable to that of non-private quantile regression models. This demonstrates that the algorithm effectively protects data privacy without compromising data usability.

title: Multidimensional Measurement Method for Surface Charge on Complex Contoured Surfaces of DC GIL Tri-Post Insulator
authors: Xuan Li, Feichen Zhang, Yichun Yin, Yuan Wang, Qingyang Huang, Qingmin Li
publish: 2025 2nd international conference on DC technologies and systems (DCTS)
abstract: In practical ultra- and extra-high-voltage DC GIL systems, severe surface charge accumulation on tri-post insulators can easily trigger the surface flashover failures. However, measurement methods for surface charge on the complex curved contours of tri-post insulators are lacking, impeding detailed study of the discharge mechanism associated with surface charge distortion. To address this issue, this paper proposes a multidimensional measurement method for surface charge on complex contoured surfaces of tri-post insulators, based on an active electrostatic probe. Furthermore, a three-dimensional apparent surface charge inversion model and an improved optimization algorithm suitable for tri-post insulator geometries were established, enabling stable and efficient calculation of surface charge from potential measurements. Experimental results reveal that positive charges tend to accumulate in the central trunk region, while negative charges gather on the supporting legs, with charge densities exceeding <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100 \mu \mathrm{C}/\mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex>. The charge density increases with both applied voltage and time, while temperature shows a gradient decay toward the enclosure, leading to significant charge enhancement at the leg bases. This study provides a feasible framework for surface charge measurement and fault diagnosis of DC GIL tri-post insulators.

title: HMTraj: A Hybrid Mamba–Transformer Framework for Efficient Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction
authors: Shiyi Tang, Kai Wu
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Intelligent Communication, Sensing and Electromagnetics (ICSE)
abstract: Accurate and efficient pedestrian trajectory prediction is essential for safe motion planning in autonomous driving and mobile robotic systems. Although deep learning models have advanced this field, achieving a balance between prediction accuracy and computational efficiency remains a significant challenge, especially when jointly modeling long-range historical dependencies lying in pedestrian trajectories and complex social interactions among agents, hindering their practical applications. To address this, we propose HMTraj, a hybrid Mamba–Transformer framework for efficient pedestrian trajectory prediction. HMTraj employs a Motion-Mamba encoder operating on a compact set of representative motion patterns to efficiently capture diverse motion behaviors. Then, a Social-Attention decoder is used to selectively attend social interactions with relevant neighboring pedestrians. Experiments on the Stanford Drone Dataset (SDD) demonstrate that our method achieves competitive accuracy while maintaining superior computational efficiency with fewer parameters and lower inference time, validating its potential for providing reliable and practical trajectory prediction in resource-constrained, real-time applications.

title: Application of the Methontology Method to the Lombok Tourism Ontology Model with Chatbot Integration Using Apache Jena Fuseki
authors: Hartono Wijaya, Helna Wardhana, Anthony Anggrawan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence, Data Engineering and Sciences (ICAIDES)
abstract: Lombok Island is recognized as one of Indonesia’s key tourist destinations, offering diverse natural landscapes, cultural heritage, and traditional practices. However, tourism information is often scattered across unrelated sources, lacks structural consistency, and becomes difficult for travelers to interpret effectively. This study proposes a semantic knowledge system supported by an ontology driven chatbot to organize and deliver Lombok tourism information more coherently. The system is developed using the Methontology framework, covering specification, knowledge acquisition, conceptual modeling, implementation, evaluation, and documentation. The ontology is built in Protege, defining essential classes, properties, and relationships representing destinations, activities, facilities, prices, and cultural elements. It is then exported into RDF/XML and deployed through Apache Jena Fuseki as a SPARQL endpoint connected to a chatbot interface. The chatbot generates context-aware responses by leveraging semantic relationships encoded in the ontology, enabling structured information retrieval tailored to user queries. Logical validation using a reasoner ensures the ontology’s consistency, while SPARQL based testing verifies accurate and reliable data extraction. The results demonstrate that the ontology based approach improves interoperability, enhances data accessibility, and supports more intelligent information delivery for Lombok tourism. This research provides a foundation for future enhancements, including multilingual capabilities, real- ime data integration, and personalized recommendation features.

title: Intelligent Ordered Escape Routing via Deep Reinforcement Learning and Hybrid Heuristics
authors: Linfeng Zeng, Xiaoyu Liang, Ning Xu, Qingjia Chi, Jiaojiao Yu, Yongkang Cai
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
abstract: This paper proposes an ordered escape routing method based on deep reinforcement learning (DRL), aiming to address the efficient connection between pins and external circuits in high-density printed circuit board (PCB) designs. Escape routing is a critical step in PCB design, especially when dealing with dense pin arrays and limited routing space, where traditional methods often struggle to meet the requirements of routing success rate and signal integrity. In this work, the ordered escape routing problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm is employed to optimize the selection of routing endpoints. Specifically, we design a DRL model capable of predicting escape endpoints and develop a hybrid algorithm that integrates the artificial potential field method with the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{A}^{\ast}$</tex> algorithm. To address the challenging issue of routing order, a Simulated Annealing (SA) algorithm is also incorporated to guide and enhance the learning process, enabling the agent to discover optimal routing strategies under complex constraints. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed DRL-based approach significantly improves routing success rate, total wire length, signal integrity, and computational efficiency compared to traditional heuristic algorithms and Simulated Annealing algorithms. For instance, in our benchmark cases, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> routing success rate was achieved, along with notable reductions in total routing length. This study provides an innovative escape routing solution for high-density PCB designs and offers new insights into the application of deep reinforcement learning in electronic design automation (EDA).

title: An Archive of Tensions: A Growing Collection of Challenges in More-Than-Human Design
authors: Judith Dörrenbächer, Rosan Chow, Daisy Yoo, Eva Eriksson
abstract: None

title: Design of a Portable Gait Profiler with Uneven Surface and Low Light Adaptability
authors: David John Saldanha, Swaminathan Meenatchi Sundaram, Manikandan Natarajan, Shreeraksha Arunachala Chandavar, Sucheta Kolekar, T R S Vishnukumar, Sandeepkumar Mandal, Gaurav Karunakara Poojary
abstract: Gait analysis is simply a way of studying how a person walks to learn about their body’s movements and posture. Individuals with conditions that affect their ability to walk, such as cerebral palsy, stroke, or Parkinson’s disease, often undergo gait analysis in a specialized laboratory. The aspects of their walking that are measured determine their specific health issues and provide the information required by clinicians and healthcare providers. While gait tests offer valuable insights, inherent challenges exist for both patients and clinicians. Patients may experience discomfort, anxiety, physical limitations, and cost and accessibility barriers. Clinicians may experience differences in equipment, protocols, and reference values between laboratories, as well as interpretation difficulties. Traditional gait assessment solely depends on observations of things such as joint angles and the range of motion (ROM) with multiple cameras in a specialized room. As patients experience physical strain because of travel and test-related procedures, they may develop stress and anxiety, potentially leading to inaccurate readings. Patients often skip the hospital because they would have to take time off work. The availability of a portable device to measure gait may open the possibility of conducting analysis and rehabilitation in a home setting and can also be a screening tool for community hospitals, which can drastically improve patient recovery. In this work, a portable and easy-to-carry gait profiler was created to overcome the challenges faced by clinicians and patients with traditional gait analysis. The device can collect data in a variety of real-world settings, such as homes, clinics, and the community, offering an alternative to costly and inaccessible lab settings. The profiler precisely captures a patient’s natural gait under actual circumstances via computer vision. Low-light compatibility, which makes use of specialized cameras and infrared emitters to guarantee precise measurements even in poorly lit areas, is one of its primary features. The gait profiler created is a reliable and practical tool for clinical and research use, as it has been demonstrated to possess the same degree of accuracy, robustness, and usefulness as standard gait laboratory tests.

title: An Efficient and Secure Self-learning Federated Learning Method based on Chaotic Secure Clustering for Data Processing in Internet of Things
authors: Yang Lan, Lixiang Li, Haipeng Peng, Yeqing Ren, Cuicui Wang
abstract: The emergence of federated learning (FL) promotes the rapid development of artificial intelligence technology in Internet of Things (IoT). However, the huge communication cost and consumption of local computing resource in the process of FL restricts its further development and application in IoT. To solve the above problems, this paper proposes an efficient and secure self-learning federated learning method based on chaotic secure clustering. Firstly, the self-learning weights selection strategy is proposed, this strategy divides FL into two stages. In the first stage, the clients randomly select partial weights according to the inactivation parameter, which are encrypted by CKKS homomorphic encryption and uploaded, and the server updates the weights adaptive matrix according to the performance of the global model. In the second stage, the clients select some weights to encrypt and upload according to the weights adaptive matrix and inactivation parameter. Then, to further improve the efficiency of FL, we propose the chaotic secure clustering method to select some clients. The server performs clustering operation before FL. And in the clustering process, we transform and locally-globally scramble the client label information, which ensures the security of the client information in the clustering process. Finally, we use MNIST dataset, fashion-MNIST dataset, GTSRB dataset and CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset to simulate non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) scenarios, and the experimental results show the effectiveness, security and efficiency of the proposed method, and our method provides a new learning paradigm for the further development and application of FL in IoT.

title: Nonlinear Bounded Error Compensation for Flux based Encoderless Controller of PMSMs
authors: Zhenxiao Yin, Fobao Zhou, Xueyan Wang, Yang Shen, Jiaqiao Liang, Xiangdong Su, Hang Zhao
abstract: The encoderless control method based on a non-linear flux observer is considered effective in the medium-to-high speed range, but it fails in the low-speed high-torque operating region due to excessive angle errors, which forces the small drone to shut down when landing. To solve this problem, this paper proposes a nonlinear angle error compensator that acts directly on the angle estimation. The mathematical model of the motor flux observer and the flux compensation term is analyzed, which are proven to have corresponding error bounds. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can operate effectively and stably in the low-speed and high-torque region where conventional methods fail, while also achieving good control performance across other speed ranges even under inaccurate motor parameters. Additionally, the theoretical relationship between observer parameters and the convergence speed after motor load disturbances has been verified in experiments. It is further confirmed that in scenarios including gradual loading, sudden heavy load at low speed, large external current disturbance, and speed ramp-down tests, the magnitude of the flux error does not exceed the error bound.

title: Cobalt Anode Quasi-Vertical GaN Schottky Barrier Diodes With Low Turn-On Voltage and Leakage Current Density
authors: Song Fu, Qun Ma, Junjie Kang, Meng Liang, Yongjie Wang, Xiaoyan Yi, Junxi Wang, Jinmin Li, Zhiqiang Liu
abstract: Gallium nitride (GaN)-based Schottky barrier diodes (SBDs) are promising candidates for replacing silicon devices in high-frequency and high-temperature applications. The performance of the metal/GaN Schottky contacts is critical to SBDs. In this study, we demonstrate a high-performance quasi-vertical GaN SBD with a cobalt (Co) anode fabricated on state-of-the-art crystal quality GaN films grown on the patterned sapphire substrates. The Co-SBD exhibits near-ideal thermionic emission (TE) transport, with an ideality factor of 1.04 and a Schottky barrier height (SBH) value of 0.74 eV. Key performance metrics of the device include low turn-on voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V} _{\mathrm {on}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of 0.48 V, low-specific differential <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R} _{\mathrm {on},\text {sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 1.3m<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega \cdot \text {cm}^{{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, high operating current of 0.96 A at 2.0 V, and high breakdown voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V} _{\text {BR}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of 245 V. Temperature-dependent current–voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$I$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) measurements reveal minimal variation in SBH and ideality factor, indicating excellent thermal stability. The comprehensive analysis of leakage mechanisms confirms that thermionic field emission (TFE) dominates at low biases and variable-range hopping (VRH) conduction at high biases. These results demonstrate Co as a promising anode material for achieving low turn-on voltage and low leakage current density in quasi-vertical GaN SBDs.

title: Deep-Learning Based Waveform Design for Single-Carrier Satellite Communication Networks
authors: Yara Huleihel, Rom Hirsch, Gregory Sominsky, Roi Ziv, Haim H. Permuter
abstract: This study introduces an innovative deep-learning-based waveform design approach for single-carrier (SC) satellite communication systems. Neural network (NN) models are used for comprehensive optimization to mitigate the nonlinearities introduced by high-power amplifiers (HPAs). We propose two distinct NN architectures: a convolutional autoencoder (CAE) for end-to-end joint optimization and a mutual information neural estimation (MINE)-based architecture designed to optimize the transmitter and receiver components independently. Both models simultaneously enhance spectral purity by improving the adjacent channel power ratio (ACPR), reducing the peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), and optimizing the bit error rate (BER). A key feature of our approach is its ability to generalize across diverse HPA models, enabling quick and effective adaptation to previously unseen amplifiers through minimal retraining. Additionally, our use of gradual loss learning allows for the effective balancing of multiple optimization objectives across various signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) scenarios. Rigorous evaluations using realistic satellite terminal block upconverter (BUC) HPA models, demonstrate the robustness and adaptability of the proposed NN solutions. A comparative analysis against conventional PAPR reduction and digital predistortion techniques confirms that our NN-based approaches deliver superior performance, addressing contemporary satellite communication demands, particularly in high-order QAM applications requiring stringent spectral constraints.

title: GGCT-Net: A Dual-Branch Gated Graph Convolution and Grouped Cross-Attention Transformer Network for Hyperspectral Unmixing
authors: Qingfei Liu, Xiaodong Yu, Hongbin Dong, Shuying Zang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, deep learning-based hyperspectral unmixing techniques have garnered increasing attention and achieved significant progress. However, existing deep learning methods exhibit notable limitations: CNNs struggle to correlate spectrally similar yet spatially dispersed pixels, while Transformers lack the ability to distinguish between spectral and spatial features when processing high-dimensional data. To address these, this article proposes a dual-branch gated graph convolution and grouped cross-attention Transformer network (GGCT-Net). Specifically, this network designs a Spatial-Spectral Cross-Attention Block (SCAB) within the Grouped Cross-Attention Transformer encoder (GCA-Transformer) to process spectral and spatial features separately. Within the gated graph convolution encoder (G-GCN), a graph structure based on spectral similarity is constructed to correlate spectrally similar yet spatially dispersed pixels, and deep feature interactions are achieved through a gated cross-attention fusion module (GCAF). Experimental results on both simulate and real hyperspectral datasets demonstrate the proposed method’s effectiveness and superiority compared to various hyperspectral unmixing approaches.
